questions.question: What is the typical context switch cost on modern hardware?
questions.correctAnswer: ~1-5 µs (microseconds)
questions.explanation: Context switch: save/restore registers, switch page tables (CR3), potentially flush TLB. ~1-5 µs on modern CPUs.

questions.question: What is SMT (Simultaneous Multithreading)?
options: One physical core appearing as two logical cores — executes two threads simultaneously, sharing execution units
questions.correctAnswer: One physical core appearing as two logical cores — executes two threads simultaneously, sharing execution units
questions.explanation: Intel Hyper-Threading = SMT. Two threads share one core's execution units. Not true parallelism but better utilization.

questions.question: What is thread affinity?
options: Pinning a thread to a specific CPU core — prevents migration, improves cache locality
questions.correctAnswer: Pinning a thread to a specific CPU core — prevents migration, improves cache locality
questions.explanation: Thread affinity binds a thread to a core. Reduces cache misses from migration. Critical for latency-sensitive threads.

questions.question: Why use a thread pool instead of creating threads on demand?
options: Avoids thread creation/destruction overhead — reuses pre-created threads, bounds resource usage
questions.correctAnswer: Avoids thread creation/destruction overhead — reuses pre-created threads, bounds resource usage
questions.explanation: Thread creation: allocate stack (~MB), kernel scheduling. Pool: keep N threads alive, dispatch work to them.

questions.question: Why might you disable SMT/Hyper-Threading for a game server?
options: SMT threads share L1/L2 cache and execution units — can cause cache thrashing and unpredictable latency for latency-sensitive workloads
questions.a: SMT threads share L1/L2 cache and execution units — can cause cache thrashing and unpredictable latency for latency-sensitive workloads
questions.e: Two threads on one core compete for cache and execution units. For latency-critical threads, dedicated cores are better.
questions.correctAnswer: No reason
questions.explanation: See the SMT, Affinity & Thread Pools note for details.

questions.question: What is the optimal thread pool size for CPU-bound work?
options: std::thread::hardware_concurrency() — typically the number of logical cores
questions.correctAnswer: std::thread::hardware_concurrency() — typically the number of logical cores
questions.explanation: For CPU-bound work: N threads = N logical cores. More threads = context switch overhead with no throughput gain.

questions.question: What is a data race in C++?
options: Two threads accessing the same memory concurrently, at least one writes, no synchronization — undefined behavior
questions.correctAnswer: Two threads accessing the same memory concurrently, at least one writes, no synchronization — undefined behavior
questions.explanation: C++ standard: concurrent access + at least one write + no synchronization = data race = UB.

questions.question: What does std::mutex::lock() do when the mutex is already locked?
options: Blocks (sleeps) until the mutex is unlocked
questions.correctAnswer: Blocks (sleeps) until the mutex is unlocked
questions.explanation: lock() blocks the calling thread. On Linux, it uses futex — fast path in userspace, slow path via kernel FUTEX_WAIT.

questions.question: What is a deadlock?
options: Thread A holds lock 1, waits for lock 2; Thread B holds lock 2, waits for lock 1 — neither can proceed
questions.correctAnswer: Thread A holds lock 1, waits for lock 2; Thread B holds lock 2, waits for lock 1 — neither can proceed
questions.explanation: Deadlock = circular wait. Four Coffman conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait.

questions.question: What does std::scoped_lock do?
options: Locks multiple mutexes atomically using deadlock-avoidance algorithm
questions.correctAnswer: Locks multiple mutexes atomically using deadlock-avoidance algorithm
questions.explanation: scoped_lock(m1, m2, m3) locks all simultaneously. If any fails, releases all. RAII: auto-unlock on scope exit.

questions.question: How does a futex (Linux) achieve fast uncontended mutex?
options: CAS on futex word in userspace — fast path ~20 cycles, no kernel involvement. Kernel only on contention.
questions.correctAnswer: CAS on futex word in userspace — fast path ~20 cycles, no kernel involvement. Kernel only on contention.
questions.explanation: futex: 0=unlocked, 1=locked-no-waiters, 2=locked-with-waiters. Lock = CAS 0→1 (~20 cycles). Contention → FUTEX_WAIT syscall.

questions.question: What is livelock?
options: A deadlock variant where threads are blocked
Threads are actively running but constantly yielding to each other — making zero net progress
questions.correctAnswer: Threads are actively running but constantly yielding to each other — making zero net progress
questions.explanation: Livelock: threads respond to each other's actions but no forward progress. Different from deadlock (threads not blocked).

questions.question: What is starvation?
options: A thread is ready but never gets the mutex because higher-priority threads keep acquiring it
questions.correctAnswer: A thread is ready but never gets the mutex because higher-priority threads keep acquiring it
questions.explanation: Starvation: low-priority thread never gets CPU or lock. Fix: fair scheduling or priority inheritance.

questions.question: What are the four Coffman conditions for deadlock?
questions.correctAnswer: Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
acceptableAnswers: mutual exclusion hold-and-wait no preemption circular wait
mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait
questions.explanation: All four must hold for deadlock. Break any one to prevent deadlock. Lock ordering breaks circular wait.

questions.question: How does consistent lock ordering prevent deadlock?
options: If all threads lock A before B, circular wait is impossible
questions.correctAnswer: If all threads lock A before B, circular wait is impossible
questions.explanation: Lock ordering eliminates the circular wait condition. Every thread acquires locks in the same order.

questions.question: What is the difference between deadlock and livelock at the CPU level?
options: Deadlock: threads in SLEEPING state (blocked). Livelock: threads in RUNNING state but making no progress.
questions.correctAnswer: Deadlock: threads in SLEEPING state (blocked). Livelock: threads in RUNNING state but making no progress.
questions.explanation: Deadlock = blocked in FUTEX_WAIT. Livelock = executing CAS loops, burning CPU, zero progress.

questions.question: What does std::atomic guarantee?
options: Atomicity — indivisible read-modify-write, no torn reads/writes, well-defined memory ordering
questions.correctAnswer: Atomicity — indivisible read-modify-write, no torn reads/writes, well-defined memory ordering
questions.explanation: atomic is lock-free for fundamental types. Guarantees: no torn reads, indivisible RMW, controlled memory ordering.

questions.question: What does memory_order_relaxed provide?
options: Atomicity only — no ordering guarantees between threads. Counters where order doesn't matter.
questions.correctAnswer: Atomicity only — no ordering guarantees between threads. Counters where order doesn't matter.
questions.explanation: relaxed = cheapest. Use for simple counters where only the final value matters, not the ordering relative to other operations.

questions.question: What does memory_order_seq_cst guarantee?
options: Single total order — all threads agree on the order of all seq_cst operations. Strongest, most expensive.
questions.correctAnswer: Single total order — all threads agree on the order of all seq_cst operations. Strongest, most expensive.
questions.explanation: seq_cst = default. MFENCE on x86 (~20-30 cycles). All threads see all seq_cst ops in the same order.

questions.question: How does acquire-release ordering work?
options: Release store synchronizes-with acquire load on same atomic — creates happens-before edge, transitively ordering all prior writes
questions.correctAnswer: Release store synchronizes-with acquire load on same atomic — creates happens-before edge, transitively ordering all prior writes
questions.explanation: acquire/release pair creates a synchronizes-with relationship. Classic pattern: flag.store(true, release); while(!flag.load(acquire)).

questions.question: Why is x86 'easy mode' for memory ordering?
options: x86 has Total Store Order (TSO) — stores appear in consistent order to all cores. acquire/release are free.
questions.correctAnswer: x86 has Total Store Order (TSO) — stores appear in consistent order to all cores. acquire/release are free.
questions.explanation: x86 TSO makes acquire/release semantically free. ARM/RISC-V are weakly ordered — require explicit barrier instructions.

questions.question: What is a condition variable used for?
options: Blocking a thread until a condition becomes true — wait() releases mutex and sleeps; notify wakes
questions.correctAnswer: Blocking a thread until a condition becomes true — wait() releases mutex and sleeps; notify wakes
questions.explanation: cv.wait(lock, predicate) atomically releases lock and sleeps. notify_one()/notify_all() wakes waiters.

questions.question: What is the producer-consumer pattern?
options: Producer thread(s) generate data; consumer thread(s) process it — synchronized via a queue with mutex+condition variable
questions.correctAnswer: Producer thread(s) generate data; consumer thread(s) process it — synchronized via a queue with mutex+condition variable
questions.explanation: Producer pushes to queue, notifies consumer. Consumer waits on CV, pops and processes. Bounded buffer variant limits queue size.

questions.question: What is a read-write lock?
options: Allows multiple simultaneous readers OR one exclusive writer — shared_mutex in C++17
questions.correctAnswer: Allows multiple simultaneous readers OR one exclusive writer — shared_mutex in C++17
questions.explanation: shared_mutex: lock_shared() for readers (concurrent), lock() for writers (exclusive). Good for read-heavy workloads.

questions.question: What is a semaphore (C++20)?
options: A counter-based synchronization primitive — acquire decrements (blocks at 0), release increments
questions.correctAnswer: A counter-based synchronization primitive — acquire decrements (blocks at 0), release increments
questions.explanation: counting_semaphore<N> limits concurrent access to N threads. Binary semaphore (N=1) is similar to mutex but not owned.

questions.question: What is a barrier (std::barrier, C++20)?
options: A synchronization point where ALL participating threads must arrive before ANY can proceed
questions.correctAnswer: A synchronization point where ALL participating threads must arrive before ANY can proceed
questions.explanation: barrier blocks until N threads have arrived. Used for phased algorithms where all threads must complete phase N before starting N+1.

questions.question: What does std::call_once ensure?
options: A function is called exactly once, even with concurrent callers — thread-safe lazy initialization
questions.correctAnswer: A function is called exactly once, even with concurrent callers — thread-safe lazy initialization
questions.explanation: call_once + once_flag = guaranteed single execution. More efficient than mutex for one-shot initialization.

questions.question: What makes an algorithm lock-free?
options: At least one thread makes progress in a bounded number of steps — no thread can block all others indefinitely
questions.correctAnswer: At least one thread makes progress in a bounded number of steps — no thread can block all others indefinitely
questions.explanation: Lock-free: some thread always makes progress. Wait-free: EVERY thread makes progress in bounded steps. Stronger guarantee.

questions.question: What is CAS (Compare-And-Swap)?
options: Atomic: if value matches expected, swap with desired — foundation of lock-free data structures
questions.correctAnswer: Atomic: if value matches expected, swap with desired — foundation of lock-free data structures
questions.explanation: compare_exchange_weak/strong: if (*ptr == expected) *ptr = desired; else expected = *ptr. CAS loop retries on failure.

questions.question: What is the ABA problem?
options: Value changes A→B→A — CAS sees A and succeeds, but the state changed in between. LL/SC on ARM detects this.
questions.correctAnswer: Value changes A→B→A — CAS sees A and succeeds, but the state changed in between. LL/SC on ARM detects this.
questions.explanation: ABA: CAS can't detect intermediate changes. ARM's Load-Linked/Store-Conditional detects ANY write between load and store.

questions.question: What is the advantage of lock-free over mutex-based code?
options: No thread can be blocked/suspended — immune to priority inversion, deadlock, and scheduler preemption mid-operation
questions.correctAnswer: No thread can be blocked/suspended — immune to priority inversion, deadlock, and scheduler preemption mid-operation
questions.explanation: Lock-free: a thread can't block others. If OS preempts a thread mid-CAS, other threads still make progress.

questions.question: What is a hazard pointer?
options: A mechanism for safe memory reclamation in lock-free data structures — prevents use-after-free without GC
questions.correctAnswer: A mechanism for safe memory reclamation in lock-free data structures — prevents use-after-free without GC
questions.explanation: Hazard pointers mark objects as 'in use' by a thread. Objects aren't freed until no thread has them hazard-marked.

questions.question: What is false sharing?
options: Two threads modify different variables that share the same cache line — cache coherence ping-pongs the line, destroying performance
questions.correctAnswer: Two threads modify different variables that share the same cache line — cache coherence ping-pongs the line, destroying performance
questions.explanation: Cache line = 64 bytes. Two independent variables on same line → false sharing. Fix: align to cache line (alignas(64)).

questions.question: When is a spinlock better than a mutex?
options: When the critical section is extremely short (a few cycles) — avoids context switch overhead for brief waits
questions.correctAnswer: When the critical section is extremely short (a few cycles) — avoids context switch overhead for brief waits
questions.explanation: Spinlock: busy-waits (burns CPU). Good for <~100 cycle critical sections. Mutex: sleeps (saves CPU). Good for longer sections.

questions.question: What is work stealing?
options: A thread with an empty queue steals work from another thread's queue — used in task schedulers for load balancing
questions.correctAnswer: A thread with an empty queue steals work from another thread's queue — used in task schedulers for load balancing
questions.explanation: Work stealing: idle threads pull tasks from busy threads' queues. Key to efficient thread pool scheduling (TBB, UE TaskGraph).

questions.question: What is thread-local storage (thread_local)?
options: Each thread gets its own independent copy of the variable — no synchronization needed
questions.correctAnswer: Each thread gets its own independent copy of the variable — no synchronization needed
questions.explanation: thread_local = per-thread instance. Ideal for allocators, RNG state, per-thread counters. Zero contention.

questions.question: What is Amdahl's Law?
options: Maximum speedup limited by the sequential fraction: speedup ≤ 1/(S + (1-S)/N) where S = sequential portion
questions.correctAnswer: Maximum speedup limited by the sequential fraction: speedup ≤ 1/(S + (1-S)/N) where S = sequential portion
questions.explanation: Amdahl: if 10% of code is sequential (S=0.1), maximum speedup with infinite cores is 10×. Diminishing returns from parallelism.

questions.question: What does std::mutex provide?
options: Mutual exclusion — lock() blocks until available, unlock() releases
questions.correctAnswer: Mutual exclusion — lock() blocks until available, unlock() releases
questions.explanation: mutex = basic mutual exclusion. Use lock_guard or unique_lock for RAII. scoped_lock for multiple mutexes.

questions.question: What does std::atomic<T> provide?
options: Lock-free atomic operations — indivisible read-modify-write with configurable memory ordering
questions.correctAnswer: Lock-free atomic operations — indivisible read-modify-write with configurable memory ordering
questions.explanation: atomic is lock-free for int, pointer, bool on all modern platforms. Use for counters, flags, lock-free structures.

questions.question: What does std::jthread (C++20) add over std::thread?
options: Auto-join on destruction and cooperative cancellation via stop_token
questions.correctAnswer: Auto-join on destruction and cooperative cancellation via stop_token
questions.explanation: jthread joins automatically in destructor (no terminate()). stop_source/stop_token enable cooperative cancellation.

questions.question: What is the recommended way to pass data between threads?
options: std::promise/std::future for one-shot; queue + mutex + condition_variable for streaming
questions.correctAnswer: std::promise/std::future for one-shot; queue + mutex + condition_variable for streaming
questions.explanation: future/promise for single value transfer. Producer-consumer queue for continuous data flow. Avoid raw shared state.

questions.question: How do you prevent data races on shared data?
options: Use std::mutex + lock_guard for mutual exclusion, OR std::atomic for simple types, OR lock-free structures
questions.correctAnswer: Use std::mutex + lock_guard for mutual exclusion, OR std::atomic for simple types, OR lock-free structures
questions.explanation: Three strategies: mutex (general, blocks), atomic (lock-free, simple types only), lock-free (complex, hard to write correctly).

questions.question: What are the three mechanical operations that dominate HDD latency?
questions.correctAnswer: Seek (move head to track), rotational latency (wait for sector to spin under head), and transfer (read data)
questions.explanation: Seek ~8-9ms, rotational ~4ms (7200 RPM), transfer ~16µs for 4KB. 99.9% of latency is mechanical.

questions.question: Why is sequential access 100-750x faster than random access on HDD?
options: Sequential: head stays on track, sectors stream by. Random: head must physically move for every 4KB
questions.correctAnswer: Sequential: head stays on track, sectors stream by. Random: head must physically move for every 4KB
questions.explanation: Sequential: 250 MB/s. Random 4KB: ~0.33 MB/s (~83 IOPS × 4KB). Ratio ~750:1. Entirely due to mechanical seek+rotation.

questions.question: What does 'Advanced Format' / 4K native sectors mean?
options: Physical sector size is 4096 bytes, not 512. Logical may still be 512 for backward compat — causes read-modify-write
questions.correctAnswer: Physical sector size is 4096 bytes, not 512. Logical may still be 512 for backward compat — causes read-modify-write
questions.explanation: 512e drives: logical 512, physical 4096. A 512-byte write triggers RMW of full 4KB sector. Align partitions to 4KB.

questions.question: What is the areal density of modern HDDs?
options: How tightly bits are packed on the platter surface — higher density = more data per rotation = higher sequential throughput
questions.correctAnswer: How tightly bits are packed on the platter surface — higher density = more data per rotation = higher sequential throughput
questions.explanation: Higher areal density = more bits pass under the head per rotation. Primary driver of HDD throughput improvements.

questions.question: Why is append-only writing fast on HDD?
options: Head stays at end of file — no seek needed. In-place update requires seeking to middle: 8ms+ vs microseconds
questions.correctAnswer: Head stays at end of file — no seek needed. In-place update requires seeking to middle: 8ms+ vs microseconds
questions.explanation: Append = sequential write at current position. In-place = seek + rotational latency. Difference: 100-1000x.

questions.question: What is the fundamental asymmetry of NAND flash?
questions.correctAnswer: Read/program per page (~4-16KB), erase per block (hundreds of pages). Cannot overwrite — must erase first.
acceptableAnswers: read program per page erase per block
read and write per page, erase per block
questions.explanation: Page = read/write unit (~25-100µs read, ~200-1500µs write). Block = erase unit (512-2048 pages, ~1.5-10ms).

questions.question: What is write amplification?
options: A 4KB user write can trigger much larger internal writes due to garbage collection — e.g., 4KB → 2MB
questions.correctAnswer: A 4KB user write can trigger much larger internal writes due to garbage collection — e.g., 4KB → 2MB
questions.explanation: Overwrite → old page stale, write to new page. GC: copy valid pages, erase block. User 4KB may cause 2MB+ of NAND writes.

questions.question: What does the Flash Translation Layer (FTL) do?
options: Maps logical block addresses (LBAs) to physical NAND pages — handles wear leveling, garbage collection, address indirection
questions.correctAnswer: Maps logical block addresses (LBAs) to physical NAND pages — handles wear leveling, garbage collection, address indirection
questions.explanation: FTL = SSD's firmware brain. LBA→physical mapping, wear leveling, GC, bad block management. OS sees a block device.

questions.question: Why does SLC endure 50-100x more writes than QLC?
options: SLC: 2 voltage levels (charged/not). QLC: 16 levels. Tiny voltage windows degrade faster with oxide wear
questions.correctAnswer: SLC: 2 voltage levels (charged/not). QLC: 16 levels. Tiny voltage windows degrade faster with oxide wear
questions.explanation: SLC: 50K-100K P/E cycles. QLC: ~500-1000. More bits per cell = tighter voltage discrimination = faster wear-out.

questions.question: Why do SSDs slow down when nearly full?
options: Few free blocks → every write triggers GC → write amplification spikes → throughput drops from GB/s to 100s MB/s
questions.correctAnswer: Few free blocks → every write triggers GC → write amplification spikes → throughput drops from GB/s to 100s MB/s
questions.explanation: 90% full SSD: few pre-erased blocks. GC must reclaim before writes. Over-provisioning (7-28% hidden spare) mitigates.

questions.question: Why is NVMe faster than SATA for SSDs?
options: NVMe: 65,536 queues × 65,536 commands, direct PCIe connection. SATA: 1 queue × 32 commands, AHCI overhead
questions.correctAnswer: NVMe: 65,536 queues × 65,536 commands, direct PCIe connection. SATA: 1 queue × 32 commands, AHCI overhead
questions.explanation: SATA designed for single-actuator HDDs (one thing at a time). NVMe designed for SSD parallelism (many NAND dies in parallel).

questions.question: What is the practical bandwidth of PCIe 4.0 ×4?
questions.correctAnswer: ~7-8 GB/s (16 GT/s × 4 lanes × 128b/130b encoding)
questions.explanation: PCIe 4.0: 16 GT/s per lane, 128b/130b → ~1.97 GB/s per lane → ~7.88 GB/s for ×4. Real NVMe ~7 GB/s after overhead.

questions.question: What does the M.2 key tell you?
options: M-key = PCIe/NVMe (up to 4 lanes). B-key = SATA. B+M = either. The notch position encodes the protocol.
questions.correctAnswer: M-key = PCIe/NVMe (up to 4 lanes). B-key = SATA. B+M = either. The notch position encodes the protocol.
questions.explanation: M.2 is just the connector form factor. Always check: NVMe M.2 (PCIe) vs SATA M.2 (600 MB/s bottleneck).

questions.question: How many PCIe lanes does a typical consumer CPU have?
options: ~20-28 (GPU takes 16, leaving 4-12 for NVMe SSDs and peripherals)
questions.correctAnswer: ~20-28 (GPU takes 16, leaving 4-12 for NVMe SSDs and peripherals)
questions.explanation: Lane budget is fixed in silicon. Populating multiple M.2 slots may disable SATA ports or bifurcate GPU ×16 to ×8.

questions.question: What is the NVMe command set advantage over AHCI?
options: ~50% fewer CPU cycles to set up an I/O operation. No legacy ATA command translation.
questions.correctAnswer: ~50% fewer CPU cycles to set up an I/O operation. No legacy ATA command translation.
questions.explanation: NVMe command set is streamlined. AHCI carries ATA legacy overhead. NVMe ≈ half the CPU cost per I/O submission.

questions.question: What is an inode in Unix filesystems?
options: A fixed-size data structure identified by number — stores file metadata (size, permissions, timestamps, block pointers)
questions.correctAnswer: A fixed-size data structure identified by number — stores file metadata (size, permissions, timestamps, block pointers)
questions.explanation: Every file/directory has one inode. Filename lives in directory entry, not the inode. Hard links = multiple directory entries → same inode.

questions.question: What does the page cache do?
options: Caches disk data in RAM — first read hits disk, subsequent reads hit cache (~100ns vs ~10ms HDD)
questions.correctAnswer: Caches disk data in RAM — first read hits disk, subsequent reads hit cache (~100ns vs ~10ms HDD)
questions.explanation: Page cache = kernel-managed DRAM caching disk blocks. Read: check cache first. Write: write to cache, mark dirty, flush async.

questions.question: What does fsync() guarantee that write() alone does not?
options: write() goes to page cache only (volatile). fsync() forces dirty pages to durable storage before returning.
questions.correctAnswer: write() goes to page cache only (volatile). fsync() forces dirty pages to durable storage before returning.
questions.explanation: write() is fast (microseconds) but data is only in RAM. Power loss = data loss. fsync() blocks until disk confirms write.

questions.question: How does journaling prevent filesystem corruption after a crash?
options: Write-ahead log: operations are journaled BEFORE modifying structures. On reboot, committed entries are replayed.
questions.correctAnswer: Write-ahead log: operations are journaled BEFORE modifying structures. On reboot, committed entries are replayed.
questions.explanation: Journal: write intent → commit → do work → mark done. Crash: replay committed entries. Inconsistent states become consistent.

questions.question: Why is 'free RAM' misleading on Linux?
options: Much of 'used' RAM is page cache — instantly evictable if an application needs it. Unused RAM IS wasted RAM.
questions.correctAnswer: Much of 'used' RAM is page cache — instantly evictable if an application needs it. Unused RAM IS wasted RAM.
questions.explanation: Page cache = 'used' but available. Kernel will evict clean pages instantly. High free RAM = you have more RAM than your working set.

questions.question: What physically stores a bit in DRAM?
options: A capacitor holding or not holding charge (1T1C cell)
questions.correctAnswer: A capacitor holding or not holding charge (1T1C cell)
questions.explanation: DRAM = 1 transistor + 1 capacitor per bit. Capacitor charged = 1, discharged = 0. Must be refreshed every ~64ms.

questions.question: Why is DRAM called 'dynamic'?
options: Capacitor charge leaks out — must be refreshed every ~64ms or data is lost. Unlike SRAM which holds state.
questions.correctAnswer: Capacitor charge leaks out — must be refreshed every ~64ms or data is lost. Unlike SRAM which holds state.
questions.explanation: Dynamic = decays. Refresh reads and rewrites every row every 64ms. ~4.5% of DRAM time lost to refresh at normal temp.

questions.question: What is the row buffer and why does it matter?
options: When a row is opened, ALL columns are read into sense amplifiers. Subsequent accesses to same row are ~14ns vs ~40ns for closed row.
questions.correctAnswer: When a row is opened, ALL columns are read into sense amplifiers. Subsequent accesses to same row are ~14ns vs ~40ns for closed row.
questions.explanation: Row buffer hit = ~14ns (column access). Row buffer miss = tRCD + CL = ~40ns (precharge + row activate + column). 3x difference.

questions.question: Why is DRAM latency ~80-100ns while L1 cache is ~1ns?
options: Physical distance (15-25cm PCB trace = ~1ns minimum), DRAM array sensing (~50ns), bus transfer overhead
questions.correctAnswer: Physical distance (15-25cm PCB trace = ~1ns minimum), DRAM array sensing (~50ns), bus transfer overhead
questions.explanation: Four reasons: distance (speed of light), DRAM array internal slowness (tRCD + CL + tRP), pin bandwidth bottleneck, bank precharge.

questions.question: What is the memory wall?
questions.correctAnswer: CPU can execute ~400 instructions in the time of one DRAM access (100ns at 4GHz). Every cache level exists to avoid hitting DRAM.
acceptableAnswers: CPU executes 400 instructions per DRAM access
CPU is much faster than RAM
the gap between CPU speed and memory latency
questions.explanation: 4 instructions/ns × 100ns = 400 instructions per DRAM access. All caching, prefetching, data-oriented design exists because of this.

questions.question: What is virtual memory's fundamental trick?
options: Every process thinks it owns ALL memory starting at address 0. The OS silently translates virtual→physical addresses.
questions.correctAnswer: Every process thinks it owns ALL memory starting at address 0. The OS silently translates virtual→physical addresses.
questions.explanation: Virtual address space = private illusion. MMU translates every access. Each process has its own page table → different physical frames.

questions.question: How many levels does x86-64 page table have?
options: 4 (PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT) — 9 bits each, 12-bit offset = 48-bit virtual address
questions.correctAnswer: 4 (PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT) — 9 bits each, 12-bit offset = 48-bit virtual address
questions.explanation: 4-level radix tree. Each level = 512 entries. 4 sequential memory accesses for one translation. TLB caches recent translations.

questions.question: What is the TLB and why is it critical?
options: Translation Lookaside Buffer — a small hardware cache (64 entries L1) for recent virtual→physical translations. ~1 cycle hit.
questions.correctAnswer: Translation Lookaside Buffer — a small hardware cache (64 entries L1) for recent virtual→physical translations. ~1 cycle hit.
questions.explanation: TLB hit = ~1 cycle. TLB miss = 4-level page walk (~50-400ns). Without TLB, the MMU would need 4 memory accesses per translation.

questions.question: How much memory does a 64-entry L1 D-TLB cover with 4KB pages?
options: 256 KB (64 × 4KB) — very small! This is why huge pages matter.
questions.correctAnswer: 256 KB (64 × 4KB) — very small! This is why huge pages matter.
questions.explanation: 64 entries × 4KB = 256KB. If your working set is larger, TLB misses dominate. Solution: 2MB huge pages → 128MB coverage.

questions.question: What are the three key reasons virtual memory exists?
questions.correctAnswer: Isolation (processes can't access each other), simplification (programs start at address 0), overcommit (OS can promise more than physical RAM)
questions.explanation: Isolation = process can't read/write other processes. Simplification = every program thinks it starts at 0. Overcommit = allocate 10GB, use 2GB.

questions.question: What are the three types of page faults?
questions.correctAnswer: Minor (soft — page in RAM, just update PTE), Major (hard — page on disk, must I/O), Invalid (no mapping — SIGSEGV)
questions.explanation: Minor: ~1-3µs, no I/O. Major: ~50-100µs (NVMe) or 5-10ms (HDD), disk I/O. Invalid: crash (null pointer, use-after-free).

questions.question: What is demand paging?
options: Physical pages allocated on FIRST access (page fault). 100MB allocation = microseconds; real cost amortized per 4KB touched.
questions.correctAnswer: Physical pages allocated on FIRST access (page fault). 100MB allocation = microseconds; real cost amortized per 4KB touched.
questions.explanation: new/malloc creates VMA entries but no physical pages. Each 4KB page faulted in on first write. OS is lazy — laziness is performance.

questions.question: What does mmap do that read() doesn't?
options: Maps a file into virtual address space — file becomes memory. Access triggers page faults that load data on-demand.
questions.correctAnswer: Maps a file into virtual address space — file becomes memory. Access triggers page faults that load data on-demand.
questions.explanation: mmap: file pages mapped into process address space. First access = major page fault → DMA from disk. No explicit read() calls.

questions.question: What is thrashing?
options: Working set exceeds physical RAM → constant page faults → system spends more time swapping than computing
questions.correctAnswer: Working set exceeds physical RAM → constant page faults → system spends more time swapping than computing
questions.explanation: Thrashing = disk I/O saturation from swap. Mouse lags, CPU idle (waiting for disk). Fix: add RAM or reduce working set.

questions.question: What does mlock()/VirtualLock() do?
options: Pins pages in physical RAM — they can never be swapped out. For latency-critical data (audio buffers, game state).
questions.correctAnswer: Pins pages in physical RAM — they can never be swapped out. For latency-critical data (audio buffers, game state).
questions.explanation: mlock prevents swap for critical pages. Tradeoff: reduces swappable pool. Lock sparingly — only what can't tolerate 10ms stall.

questions.question: How much memory does a 64-entry TLB cover with 2MB huge pages?
questions.correctAnswer: 128 MB (64 × 2MB) — 512× more than 4KB pages
questions.explanation: 2MB pages = 21 offset bits (vs 12). One TLB entry covers 512× more. 64 entries × 2MB = 128MB. 1GB pages → 64GB coverage.

questions.question: What is NUMA and why does it matter?
options: Non-Uniform Memory Access — on multi-socket systems, each CPU has local RAM (~100ns) and remote RAM (~150-250ns)
questions.correctAnswer: Non-Uniform Memory Access — on multi-socket systems, each CPU has local RAM (~100ns) and remote RAM (~150-250ns)
questions.explanation: Multi-socket: each CPU socket has its own memory controller. Local access fast, remote access 1.5-2.5× slower via inter-socket link.

questions.question: What is Linux's first-touch memory allocation policy?
options: Physical page allocated from the NUMA node of the thread that FIRST writes to it — placement depends on which thread touches it
questions.correctAnswer: Physical page allocated from the NUMA node of the thread that FIRST writes to it — placement depends on which thread touches it
questions.explanation: First-touch: if main thread allocates on socket 0, then worker on socket 1 accesses → ALL accesses are remote (slow). Touch from correct thread.

questions.question: What is the OOM Killer?
options: When kernel can't satisfy allocation (no RAM, no swap), it kills a process based on oom_score heuristics
questions.correctAnswer: When kernel can't satisfy allocation (no RAM, no swap), it kills a process based on oom_score heuristics
questions.explanation: OOM Killer = last resort. Assigns oom_score (higher = more likely to die). Kills highest-scoring process. No negotiation.

questions.question: What is memory overcommit on Linux?
options: malloc succeeds even when RAM+swap can't cover the request — kernel promises memory, only commits on first write
questions.correctAnswer: malloc succeeds even when RAM+swap can't cover the request — kernel promises memory, only commits on first write
questions.explanation: vm.overcommit_memory=0 (heuristic). malloc(10GB) on 512MB machine → non-NULL! OOM Killer handles when you actually use it.

questions.question: What are the stages of compilation in order?
questions.correctAnswer: Preprocessor, Compiler (front-end, optimizer, back-end), Assembler, Linker
questions.explanation: .cpp → preprocessor (.i) → compiler: front-end (AST), optimizer (IR), back-end (assembly) → assembler (.o) → linker (executable).

questions.question: What does the compiler front-end do?
options: Lexing, parsing, semantic analysis — produces AST and IR from C++ source
questions.correctAnswer: Lexing, parsing, semantic analysis — produces AST and IR from C++ source
questions.explanation: Front-end: lexer → tokens, parser → AST, semantic analysis → typed AST, IR generation. This is where C++ syntax errors are caught.

questions.question: What does the optimizer do?
options: Transforms IR to faster equivalent: inlining, dead code elimination, loop unrolling, constant folding, vectorization
questions.correctAnswer: Transforms IR to faster equivalent: inlining, dead code elimination, loop unrolling, constant folding, vectorization
questions.explanation: Optimizer works on IR (GIMPLE/LLVM IR). Most passes are target-independent. -O0 skips most; -O2 is standard; -O3 is aggressive.

questions.question: What does the linker do?
options: Resolves symbols: connects each 'require' (undefined symbol) to a 'provide' (definition) across .o files and libraries
questions.correctAnswer: Resolves symbols: connects each 'require' (undefined symbol) to a 'provide' (definition) across .o files and libraries
questions.explanation: Each .o has symbol table: exports (defines) and imports (references). Linker matches them. Static: copies code. Dynamic: PLT/GOT stubs.

questions.question: What is an object file (.o)?
options: Binary machine code + symbol table + relocation entries — not yet executable
questions.correctAnswer: Binary machine code + symbol table + relocation entries — not yet executable
questions.explanation: .o contains compiled code but unresolved symbols. Linker combines .o files, resolves relocations, produces executable.

questions.question: What is the difference between static and dynamic linking?
options: Static: library code copied into executable at build time. Dynamic: library loaded at runtime, shared across processes
questions.correctAnswer: Static: library code copied into executable at build time. Dynamic: library loaded at runtime, shared across processes
questions.explanation: Static: larger binary, no runtime dependency. Dynamic: smaller binary, shared .so/.dll, can update library without recompiling.

questions.question: What are PLT and GOT in dynamic linking?
options: Procedure Linkage Table and Global Offset Table — lazy binding: first call resolves address via dynamic linker, subsequent calls jump directly
questions.correctAnswer: Procedure Linkage Table and Global Offset Table — lazy binding: first call resolves address via dynamic linker, subsequent calls jump directly
questions.explanation: PLT stub → GOT entry. First call: GOT points to dynamic linker (resolve). After: GOT updated to actual function address. Lazy binding.

questions.question: What is ASLR?
options: Address Space Layout Randomization — OS randomizes base addresses of stack, heap, libraries each execution to prevent exploits
questions.correctAnswer: Address Space Layout Randomization — OS randomizes base addresses of stack, heap, libraries each execution to prevent exploits
questions.explanation: ASLR = security through unpredictability. Attacker can't hardcode addresses. PIE (Position Independent Executable) required.

questions.question: What is an ELF file?
options: Executable and Linkable Format — the standard binary format on Linux (like PE on Windows, Mach-O on macOS)
questions.correctAnswer: Executable and Linkable Format — the standard binary format on Linux (like PE on Windows, Mach-O on macOS)
questions.explanation: ELF contains: header, program headers (segments for loader), section headers (.text, .data, .bss, .rodata), symbol tables.

questions.question: What is relocation?
options: The linker adjusting addresses in machine code to reflect the final layout in memory — fixing up references between .o files
questions.correctAnswer: The linker adjusting addresses in machine code to reflect the final layout in memory — fixing up references between .o files
questions.explanation: .o files have relocation entries: 'this address needs to point to symbol X'. Linker patches these with final addresses.

questions.question: What is a syscall?
options: A controlled transition from user mode (ring 3) to kernel mode (ring 0) — the only way user code accesses hardware/OS services
questions.correctAnswer: A controlled transition from user mode (ring 3) to kernel mode (ring 0) — the only way user code accesses hardware/OS services
questions.explanation: Syscall = user→kernel transition. Examples: read(), write(), open(), mmap(). syscall instruction on x86-64. Cost: ~100-300 cycles.

questions.question: What are CPU protection rings?
options: Privilege levels: ring 0 (kernel, full access), ring 3 (user, restricted). Syscalls transition 3→0.
questions.correctAnswer: Privilege levels: ring 0 (kernel, full access), ring 3 (user, restricted). Syscalls transition 3→0.
questions.explanation: x86 has 4 rings but only 0 and 3 are used. Ring 0: execute privileged instructions, access all memory. Ring 3: restricted.

questions.question: How does the SYSCALL instruction work?
options: Atomically: saves RIP to RCX, loads RIP from LSTAR MSR (kernel handler), switches to ring 0. SYSRET reverses.
questions.correctAnswer: Atomically: saves RIP to RCX, loads RIP from LSTAR MSR (kernel handler), switches to ring 0. SYSRET reverses.
questions.explanation: SYSCALL: RCX←RIP, RIP←LSTAR, R11←RFLAGS, RFLAGS←RFLAGS & ~FMASK. No stack switch (uses MSRs). ~100-300 cycles.

questions.question: Why are syscalls expensive?
options: Mode switch (ring 3→0), register save/restore, kernel validation of arguments, potentially TLB flush, Spectre mitigations
questions.correctAnswer: Mode switch (ring 3→0), register save/restore, kernel validation of arguments, potentially TLB flush, Spectre mitigations
questions.explanation: ~100-300 cycles minimum. More with KPTI (Meltdown mitigation: page tables switch). VDSO maps some syscalls to userspace for speed.

questions.question: What is io_uring (Linux)?
options: A high-performance async I/O interface using shared ring buffers — submit and complete queues mapped in userspace, minimal syscalls
questions.correctAnswer: A high-performance async I/O interface using shared ring buffers — submit and complete queues mapped in userspace, minimal syscalls
questions.explanation: io_uring: submission queue (SQ) and completion queue (CQ) shared between kernel and userspace. Batch I/O ops with minimal syscall overhead.

questions.question: What does the call instruction do?
options: Pushes return address (RIP_next) onto stack and jumps to function address
questions.correctAnswer: Pushes return address (RIP_next) onto stack and jumps to function address
questions.explanation: call = push return address + jmp. ret = pop RIP. Pure hardware — no OS, no heap, no allocation.

questions.question: What register points to the top of the stack?
questions.correctAnswer: RSP (Stack Pointer)
questions.explanation: RSP points to the current stack top (lowest active address). push decrements RSP; pop increments it. Stack grows downward.

questions.question: What is a stack frame?
options: The region of stack used by one function call — contains return address, saved RBP, local variables
questions.correctAnswer: The region of stack used by one function call — contains return address, saved RBP, local variables
questions.explanation: Prologue creates frame (push rbp; mov rbp,rsp; sub rsp,N). Epilogue destroys it (mov rsp,rbp; pop rbp; ret). RBP = fixed anchor.

questions.question: What is the System V AMD64 calling convention?
options: Linux/macOS ABI: first 6 integer args in RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9. Return in RAX. Red zone below RSP.
questions.correctAnswer: Linux/macOS ABI: first 6 integer args in RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9. Return in RAX. Red zone below RSP.
questions.explanation: System V: 6 register args, red zone (128 bytes below RSP usable without adjustment). Microsoft x64: 4 register args, shadow space.

questions.question: What is the difference between caller-saved and callee-saved registers?
options: Caller-saved: callee can clobber freely (RAX, RCX, RDX, R8-R11). Callee-saved: must preserve (RBX, RBP, R12-R15).
questions.correctAnswer: Caller-saved: callee can clobber freely (RAX, RCX, RDX, R8-R11). Callee-saved: must preserve (RBX, RBP, R12-R15).
questions.explanation: Caller-saved = 'if you need this value after the call, save it yourself.' Callee-saved = 'I promise not to change this.'

questions.question: What three registers matter most for function calls?
questions.correctAnswer: RIP (instruction pointer — next instruction), RSP (stack pointer — top of stack), RBP (base pointer — frame anchor)
acceptableAnswers: instruction pointer, stack pointer, base pointer
questions.explanation: RIP: what to execute next. RSP: current stack top. RBP: fixed reference within frame. RSP moves; RBP stays fixed.

questions.question: What does the function prologue do?
options: push rbp; mov rbp,rsp; sub rsp,N — saves caller's frame pointer, establishes new base, allocates locals
questions.correctAnswer: push rbp; mov rbp,rsp; sub rsp,N — saves caller's frame pointer, establishes new base, allocates locals
questions.explanation: Prologue = enter function. push rbp saves old frame. mov rbp,rsp sets new frame. sub rsp,N allocates N bytes for locals.

questions.question: What does the function epilogue do?
options: mov rsp,rbp; pop rbp; ret — deallocates locals, restores caller's frame, pops return address
questions.correctAnswer: mov rsp,rbp; pop rbp; ret — deallocates locals, restores caller's frame, pops return address
questions.explanation: Epilogue = leave function. reverse of prologue. leave = mov rsp,rbp + pop rbp (one byte vs three).

questions.question: Why is stack allocation so fast?
options: Single sub instruction on RSP — no syscall, no malloc, no GC. Deallocation = add. One cycle.
questions.correctAnswer: Single sub instruction on RSP — no syscall, no malloc, no GC. Deallocation = add. One cycle.
questions.explanation: sub rsp, 4096 = allocate 4KB in one instruction. add rsp, 4096 = free. Compare: malloc(4096) = potentially syscall + page table updates.

questions.question: What is frame pointer omission (-fomit-frame-pointer)?
options: RBP freed as general-purpose register (~6% more GPRs). Locals accessed via RSP offsets. DWARF unwind tables needed for debugging.
questions.correctAnswer: RBP freed as general-purpose register (~6% more GPRs). Locals accessed via RSP offsets. DWARF unwind tables needed for debugging.
questions.explanation: -O2 default. RBP becomes general register. Debugger can't follow RBP chain — needs .eh_frame metadata. Release builds harder to debug.

questions.question: Why must RSP be 16-byte aligned at call?
options: SSE movaps requires 16-byte aligned memory — faults on misaligned addresses. ABI mandates alignment.
questions.correctAnswer: SSE movaps requires 16-byte aligned memory — faults on misaligned addresses. ABI mandates alignment.
questions.explanation: call pushes 8 bytes (return address), misaligning to 8 mod 16. push rbp adds 8, realigning to 16. Locals rounded to 16-byte multiples.

questions.question: What is the red zone (System V only)?
options: 128 bytes BELOW RSP that the current function can use without adjusting RSP — signal handlers skip it
questions.correctAnswer: 128 bytes BELOW RSP that the current function can use without adjusting RSP — signal handlers skip it
questions.explanation: Red zone = System V only. Windows has NO red zone — below RSP is volatile (interrupts may overwrite). Cross-platform code: never assume it.

questions.question: What is the shadow space (Microsoft x64)?
options: Caller MUST allocate 32 bytes on stack before call — callee may spill its 4 register args there
questions.correctAnswer: Caller MUST allocate 32 bytes on stack before call — callee may spill its 4 register args there
questions.explanation: Shadow space = 32 bytes reserved by caller. Simplifies variadic functions and debugging. System V has no shadow space.

questions.question: How does System V differ from MS x64 for argument registers?
options: SysV: RDI,RSI,RDX,RCX,R8,R9 (6 regs). MS: RCX,RDX,R8,R9 (4 regs). SysV has red zone; MS has shadow space.
questions.correctAnswer: SysV: RDI,RSI,RDX,RCX,R8,R9 (6 regs). MS: RCX,RDX,R8,R9 (4 regs). SysV has red zone; MS has shadow space.
questions.explanation: Key differences: 6 vs 4 arg registers, RDI/RSI callee-saved on Windows (caller-saved on SysV — #1 porting trap), red zone vs shadow space.

questions.question: Why does Windows x64 use only 4 argument registers vs System V's 6?
options: Historical continuity with x86 __fastcall (ECX,EDX). Shadow space handles spill. Different design tradeoffs.
questions.correctAnswer: Historical continuity with x86 __fastcall (ECX,EDX). Shadow space handles spill. Different design tradeoffs.
questions.explanation: MS extended x86 __fastcall. AMD defined SysV ABI for 64-bit; Linux/macOS adopted it. Microsoft created their own. Historical accident.

questions.question: What is a stack buffer overflow?
options: Writing past the end of a stack buffer overwrites saved RBP and return address — attacker controls RIP on ret
questions.correctAnswer: Writing past the end of a stack buffer overwrites saved RBP and return address — attacker controls RIP on ret
questions.explanation: Buffer at [RBP-N], saved RBP at [RBP], return address at [RBP+8]. Overflow upward overwrites return address. ret → attacker code.

questions.question: What is a stack canary?
options: A random value placed between locals and saved RBP — checked before ret. If corrupted (overflow) → abort.
questions.correctAnswer: A random value placed between locals and saved RBP — checked before ret. If corrupted (overflow) → abort.
questions.explanation: Canary = compiler-inserted guard value. Overwrite buffer → corrupt canary → detected at epilogue check. Bypassable if canary leaked.

questions.question: What is ROP (Return-Oriented Programming)?
options: Chaining existing code snippets (gadgets ending in ret) to execute arbitrary logic — bypasses NX/DEP
questions.correctAnswer: Chaining existing code snippets (gadgets ending in ret) to execute arbitrary logic — bypasses NX/DEP
questions.explanation: NX bit makes stack non-executable. ROP reuses existing .text code instead of injecting new code. Gadget = short instruction sequence + ret.

questions.question: What protects against ROP at the hardware level?
options: Intel CET Shadow Stack — CPU maintains hardware-protected copy of return addresses; ret compares against it
questions.correctAnswer: Intel CET Shadow Stack — CPU maintains hardware-protected copy of return addresses; ret compares against it
questions.explanation: Shadow stack = hardware-enforced. Every call pushes to shadow stack; ret pops and compares. Mismatch → #CP fault. Hard to bypass.

questions.question: What is Tail Call Optimization (TCO)?
options: Converting call to jmp when the call is in tail position — reuses current frame, constant stack depth
questions.correctAnswer: Converting call to jmp when the call is in tail position — reuses current frame, constant stack depth
questions.explanation: Tail call = last operation before return. Compiler: call → jmp. Same frame reused. Infinite recursion becomes safe (constant stack).

questions.question: Why are 4x4 matrices used for 3D transforms?
options: Homogeneous coordinates enable translation as a linear operation — without the 4th row/column, translation requires separate vector addition
questions.correctAnswer: Homogeneous coordinates enable translation as a linear operation — without the 4th row/column, translation requires separate vector addition
questions.explanation: 4x4 matrix: (x,y,z,1) × M. The 4th column encodes translation. 3x3 can't represent translation in a single matrix multiply.

questions.question: What is the order of the MVP transformation?
options: Model → World, World → View, View → Projection (M first, then V, then P)
questions.correctAnswer: Model → World, World → View, View → Projection (M first, then V, then P)
questions.explanation: MVP = Projection × View × Model. Applied right-to-left to vertex: P·V·M·v. Model: local→world. View: world→camera. Projection: camera→clip.

questions.question: Why is matrix multiplication not commutative?
options: Order matters — rotate then translate ≠ translate then rotate. AB ≠ BA for matrices.
questions.correctAnswer: Order matters — rotate then translate ≠ translate then rotate. AB ≠ BA for matrices.
questions.explanation: Matrix multiplication is composition of transformations. Rotating then translating moves in the rotated direction. Translating then rotating moves first, then rotates around origin.

questions.question: What does the inverse of a rotation matrix equal?
options: Its transpose — rotation matrices are orthogonal: R⁻¹ = R^T
questions.correctAnswer: Its transpose — rotation matrices are orthogonal: R⁻¹ = R^T
questions.explanation: Rotation matrices are orthonormal: columns are unit vectors, mutually perpendicular. R^T × R = I. Computing transpose is cheaper than full inverse.

questions.e: A matrix's columns ARE its basis vectors. Applying a matrix to a vector expresses it in that matrix's coordinate system.
questions.explanation: A matrix's columns ARE its basis vectors. The first column is where (1,0,0) goes; the second is where (0,1,0) goes; the third is where (0,0,1) goes. Applying the matrix transforms vectors into the coordinate system defined by these basis vectors.
questions.question: What is a basis vector?
options: The axes defining a coordinate system — (1,0,0), (0,1,0), (0,0,1) in standard basis. Changing basis = changing coordinate system.
questions.correctAnswer: The axes defining a coordinate system — (1,0,0), (0,1,0), (0,0,1) in standard basis. Changing basis = changing coordinate system.

questions.question: What problem do quaternions solve that Euler angles don't?
options: Gimbal lock — when two rotation axes align, you lose a degree of freedom. Quaternions avoid this entirely.
questions.correctAnswer: Gimbal lock — when two rotation axes align, you lose a degree of freedom. Quaternions avoid this entirely.
questions.explanation: Gimbal lock: pitch=90° → yaw and roll become the same rotation. Quaternions use 4D hypersphere — no singularities.

questions.question: How many components does a quaternion have?
options: 4 (w, x, y, z) — scalar part w + vector part (x,y,z). Unit quaternion constraint: w²+x²+y²+z²=1
questions.correctAnswer: 4 (w, x, y, z) — scalar part w + vector part (x,y,z). Unit quaternion constraint: w²+x²+y²+z²=1
questions.explanation: Quaternion = cos(θ/2) + sin(θ/2)(x·i + y·j + z·k). 4 numbers, 1 constraint = 3 DOF for rotation. Same as rotation matrix but compact.

questions.question: What is SLERP?
options: Spherical Linear intERPolation — interpolates along the great circle on the 4D hypersphere, constant angular velocity
questions.correctAnswer: Spherical Linear intERPolation — interpolates along the great circle on the 4D hypersphere, constant angular velocity
questions.explanation: slerp(q0,q1,t) = (sin((1-t)Ω)/sin(Ω))·q0 + (sin(tΩ)/sin(Ω))·q1. Stays on unit hypersphere. Constant speed rotation.

questions.question: Why not just lerp quaternions?
options: lerp goes through interior of hypersphere — produces non-unit quaternions. Normalizing after (nlerp) fixes magnitude but not speed.
questions.correctAnswer: lerp goes through interior of hypersphere — produces non-unit quaternions. Normalizing after (nlerp) fixes magnitude but not speed.
questions.explanation: Lerp cuts chord through sphere. Nlerp normalizes but speeds up in middle. Slerp stays on surface at constant angular velocity.

questions.question: How are quaternions related to rotation matrices?
options: A unit quaternion q can be converted to a 3x3 rotation matrix. The conversion formula involves q's components in a specific pattern.
questions.correctAnswer: A unit quaternion q can be converted to a 3x3 rotation matrix. The conversion formula involves q's components in a specific pattern.
questions.explanation: Quaternion→matrix: standard formula. Quaternion rotation: v' = q·v·q⁻¹ (sandwich product). Two quat multiplies vs 9 multiplies + 6 adds for matrix×vector.

questions.question: What does the dot product a·b compute geometrically?
options: |a||b|cos(θ) — the projection of a onto b, times b's length. Measures how parallel they are.
questions.correctAnswer: |a||b|cos(θ) — the projection of a onto b, times b's length. Measures how parallel they are.
questions.explanation: a·b > 0 = acute angle. a·b = 0 = perpendicular. a·b < 0 = obtuse. Used everywhere: lighting (N·L), projection, angle measurement.

questions.question: What does the cross product a×b compute?
options: A vector perpendicular to both a and b, magnitude = |a||b|sin(θ) (area of parallelogram). Direction: right-hand rule.
questions.correctAnswer: A vector perpendicular to both a and b, magnitude = |a||b|sin(θ) (area of parallelogram). Direction: right-hand rule.
questions.explanation: Cross product: normal vector computation, torque, angular momentum. Only defined in 3D (and 7D). Wedge product generalizes to any dimension.

questions.question: What is the relationship between sin, cos and the unit circle?
options: (cos θ, sin θ) is a point on the unit circle at angle θ from the x-axis
questions.correctAnswer: (cos θ, sin θ) is a point on the unit circle at angle θ from the x-axis
questions.explanation: Unit circle: radius=1. cos = x-coordinate, sin = y-coordinate. cos² + sin² = 1 (Pythagorean identity). Foundation of all trigonometry.

questions.question: What are radians and why are they used?
options: Natural angle measure: 1 radian = arc length equals radius. 2π rad = 360°. Makes calculus clean (derivative of sin is cos).
questions.correctAnswer: Natural angle measure: 1 radian = arc length equals radius. 2π rad = 360°. Makes calculus clean (derivative of sin is cos).
questions.explanation: In radians: d/dx sin(x) = cos(x). In degrees: d/dx sin(x°) = (π/180)cos(x°). Radians make the math elegant. Game engines use both.

questions.e: v̂ = v/|v|. Used for direction vectors, surface normals, lighting. Normalizing zero vector = undefined (avoid).
questions.explanation: v̂ = v/|v| produces a unit vector (magnitude=1) pointing in the same direction. Used for direction vectors, surface normals, light directions, and any case where only orientation matters, not magnitude. Normalizing the zero vector is undefined (avoid).
questions.question: What is vector normalization?
options: Dividing a vector by its magnitude to produce a unit vector (length=1) — preserves direction
questions.correctAnswer: Dividing a vector by its magnitude to produce a unit vector (length=1) — preserves direction

questions.question: What is interpolation?
options: Finding intermediate values between two known data points — filling in the gaps between keyframes
questions.correctAnswer: Finding intermediate values between two known data points — filling in the gaps between keyframes
questions.explanation: Interpolation reconstructs continuous motion from discrete samples. Every frame of animation is interpolated between keyframes.

questions.question: What is the difference between lerp and easing functions?
options: Lerp = linear (constant speed). Easing = reshapes t before lerp to create acceleration/deceleration (more natural motion).
questions.correctAnswer: Lerp = linear (constant speed). Easing = reshapes t before lerp to create acceleration/deceleration (more natural motion).
questions.explanation: Lerp: A + (B-A)·t (straight line). Easing: apply function f(t) first → Lerp(A, B, f(t)). t²=ease-in, 1-(1-t)²=ease-out.

questions.question: What is Inverse Kinematics (IK)?
options: Given end effector position, solve for joint angles — 'put the hand HERE, figure out elbow and shoulder'
questions.correctAnswer: Given end effector position, solve for joint angles — 'put the hand HERE, figure out elbow and shoulder'
questions.explanation: IK: target-driven. FK: joint-driven. CCD and FABRIK are two IK algorithms. Used for foot placement, hand reaching, look-at.

questions.e: Spline = multiple curve pieces with continuity constraints at joints. C0=connected, C1=smooth tangent, C2=smooth curvature.
questions.explanation: A spline is multiple polynomial curve segments joined at knots with continuity constraints. C⁰ = connected, C¹ = smooth tangent, C² = smooth curvature. B-splines achieve C².
questions.question: What is a spline?
options: A piecewise curve made of multiple polynomial segments joined smoothly — like a flexible ruler bent through control points
questions.correctAnswer: A piecewise curve made of multiple polynomial segments joined smoothly — like a flexible ruler bent through control points

questions.e: Most curves: constant Δt ≠ constant distance. Arc-length: precompute cumulative length table, invert to get t(s). Constant speed motion.
questions.explanation: Arc-length parameterization precomputes a table of t→cumulative arc length via numerical integration of |B'(t)|. At runtime, invert via binary search to find t(s) for constant-speed traversal.
questions.question: What is arc-length parameterization?
options: Re-parameterizing a curve so equal steps in t produce equal distance traveled — fixes speed non-uniformity
questions.correctAnswer: Re-parameterizing a curve so equal steps in t produce equal distance traveled — fixes speed non-uniformity

questions.question: What defines a cubic Bezier curve?
options: 4 control points — endpoints (P0,P3) the curve passes through, handles (P1,P2) that pull the curve
questions.correctAnswer: 4 control points — endpoints (P0,P3) the curve passes through, handles (P1,P2) that pull the curve
questions.explanation: Cubic Bezier: B(t) = (1-t)³P0 + 3(1-t)²tP1 + 3(1-t)t²P2 + t³P3. Tangent at t=0: 3(P1-P0). Tangent at t=1: 3(P3-P2).

questions.question: What is the advantage of Catmull-Rom over Bezier?
options: Catmull-Rom passes THROUGH all control points (interpolating). Bezier points are 'magnets' — curve doesn't pass through handles.
questions.correctAnswer: Catmull-Rom passes THROUGH all control points (interpolating). Bezier points are 'magnets' — curve doesn't pass through handles.
questions.explanation: Catmull-Rom = interpolating spline. Each segment uses 4 points, passes through middle two. Tangent at P_i = (P_i+1 - P_i-1)/2.

questions.question: What is de Casteljau's algorithm?
options: Recursive linear interpolation to evaluate Bezier curves — numerically stable, avoids computing Bernstein polynomials
questions.correctAnswer: Recursive linear interpolation to evaluate Bezier curves — numerically stable, avoids computing Bernstein polynomials
questions.explanation: de Casteljau: repeatedly lerp between adjacent control points until one remains. O(n²) but numerically stable. Geometric intuition: fold paper.

questions.e: B-splines: don't necessarily pass through control points. Local control = editing one CV changes only local region. NURBS adds weights.
questions.explanation: B-splines use overlapping basis functions defined over a knot vector. Moving one control point only affects nearby curve segments (local control), and the curve is C² continuous at knots — smoother than Catmull-Rom (C¹).
questions.question: What is a B-spline?
options: Basis spline — generalization with local control (moving one point affects only nearby segments) and C² continuity
questions.correctAnswer: Basis spline — generalization with local control (moving one point affects only nearby segments) and C² continuity

questions.e: Speed = |dB/dt|. This is not constant. Arc-length reparameterization fixes this by driving t as a function of desired distance.
questions.explanation: The derivative magnitude |B'(t)| varies across t. Near control points the curve may move slowly; between them it may speed up. Arc-length reparameterization measures cumulative distance and maps it to parameter t.
questions.question: Why is uniform t-stepping not constant speed along a Bezier?
options: |B'(t)| varies with t — the derivative magnitude changes, so equal Δt covers different distances
questions.correctAnswer: |B'(t)| varies with t — the derivative magnitude changes, so equal Δt covers different distances

questions.question: Why does Slerp use sin functions in its formula?
options: It's computing the geodesic on S³ — the weights come from spherical trigonometry: sin((1-t)Ω)/sin(Ω)
questions.correctAnswer: It's computing the geodesic on S³ — the weights come from spherical trigonometry: sin((1-t)Ω)/sin(Ω)
questions.explanation: slerp = great circle arc on quaternion hypersphere. Weights ensure constant angular velocity. When Ω→0, sin(Ω)→0 — fall back to nlerp.

questions.question: What is CCD (Cyclic Coordinate Descent) in IK?
options: Iterative IK: rotate each joint from end-effector toward root to point at target. Simple but can produce unnatural poses.
questions.correctAnswer: Iterative IK: rotate each joint from end-effector toward root to point at target. Simple but can produce unnatural poses.
questions.explanation: CCD: for each joint (distal→proximal), rotate to minimize angle between ToEnd and ToTarget. Linear convergence. Can over-rotate proximal joints.

questions.question: What is FABRIK and how does it differ from CCD?
options: Forward And Backward Reaching IK — enforces distance constraints without computing angles. Forward pass: reach to target. Backward: anchor base.
questions.correctAnswer: Forward And Backward Reaching IK — enforces distance constraints without computing angles. Forward pass: reach to target. Backward: anchor base.
questions.explanation: FABRIK: no trig functions. Position-based. Forward: pull tip to target, adjust joints. Backward: anchor base, readjust. More natural poses than CCD.

questions.e: Quaternions double-cover SO(3). q and -q = same rotation. Slerp without check might take the LONG way around the hypersphere.
questions.explanation: q and -q represent the same rotation (quaternions double-cover SO(3)). Before slerp, compute dot product: if negative, negate one quaternion. This ensures the shorter arc is taken — without this, slerp might go the long way around the hypersphere.
questions.question: What is the double-cover problem with quaternions?
options: q and -q represent the same rotation. Before slerp, check dot product — if negative, negate one to take shorter arc.
questions.correctAnswer: q and -q represent the same rotation. Before slerp, check dot product — if negative, negate one to take shorter arc.

questions.e: FK: angles→position (deterministic). IK: position→angles (underdetermined, needs solver). FK is trivial; IK is the hard problem.
questions.explanation: FK computes end effector position from joint angles — deterministic, one solution. IK computes joint angles from end effector position — underdetermined (multiple solutions), requires iterative solver like CCD or FABRIK. FK is forward; IK is inverse.
questions.question: What is Forward Kinematics (FK)?
options: Joint-driven: specify joint angles, compute end effector position. Like controlling a puppet joint-by-joint.
questions.correctAnswer: Joint-driven: specify joint angles, compute end effector position. Like controlling a puppet joint-by-joint.

questions.question: What is Linear Blend Skinning (LBS)?
options: Weighted blend of bone transforms per vertex: v' = Σ w_i·M_i·IB_i·v. Standard GPU skinning.
questions.correctAnswer: Weighted blend of bone transforms per vertex: v' = Σ w_i·M_i·IB_i·v. Standard GPU skinning.
questions.explanation: Each vertex influenced by 1-4 bones. Bone weights sum to 1. GPU blends 4 matrices per vertex. Fast but causes candy-wrapper effect.

questions.question: What is the candy-wrapper effect in LBS?
options: When a joint twists, linear blend of rotation matrices produces non-rigid transforms — mesh collapses inward at joint
questions.correctAnswer: When a joint twists, linear blend of rotation matrices produces non-rigid transforms — mesh collapses inward at joint
questions.explanation: Cause: SO(3) not closed under addition. Blending two rotation matrices does NOT produce a rotation matrix. Determinant < 1 = volume collapse.

questions.question: How do dual quaternions fix the candy-wrapper effect?
options: Dual quaternion blend + normalization always produces rigid transform — rotation+translation, determinant=1, no volume loss
questions.correctAnswer: Dual quaternion blend + normalization always produces rigid transform — rotation+translation, determinant=1, no volume loss
questions.explanation: DQS: convert bone transforms to dual quaternions, blend, normalize. Result always rigid. ~2× cost of LBS. UE supports both.

questions.question: What is the bind pose?
options: The skeleton pose the mesh was modeled in — the reference configuration for skinning
questions.correctAnswer: The skeleton pose the mesh was modeled in — the reference configuration for skinning
questions.explanation: Bind pose = rest pose. Inverse bind matrices (IB_i) map vertices to bone-local space IN the bind pose. Computed once at import.

questions.question: How many bones typically influence a single vertex?
options: 4 (with MAX_BONE_INFLUENCES). Weights sum to 1. Extra influences dropped if > limit.
questions.correctAnswer: 4 (with MAX_BONE_INFLUENCES). Weights sum to 1. Extra influences dropped if > limit.
questions.explanation: UE default: 4 influences per vertex. 8 possible with higher quality settings. Artists paint weights in Maya/Blender. Red=strong, blue=weak.

questions.question: What does the Fourier Transform convert?
options: Time-domain signal to frequency-domain — reveals which frequencies (pitches) are present
questions.correctAnswer: Time-domain signal to frequency-domain — reveals which frequencies (pitches) are present
questions.explanation: DFT: X[k] = Σx[n]·e^(-i·2π·k·n/N). Maps N time samples to N frequency bins. Magnitude = amplitude, angle = phase.

questions.question: Why is the FFT O(N log N) instead of O(N²)?
options: Cooley-Tukey: decomposes DFT of size N into two DFTs of size N/2 recursively — divide and conquer
questions.correctAnswer: Cooley-Tukey: decomposes DFT of size N into two DFTs of size N/2 recursively — divide and conquer
questions.explanation: X[k] = E[k] + W_N^k·O[k]; X[k+N/2] = E[k] - W_N^k·O[k]. Butterfly operations. log₂(N) stages. N=1024: DFT=1M ops, FFT=10K ops.

questions.question: How does the convolution theorem enable fast reverb?
options: Time convolution = frequency multiplication. FFT both signals, multiply, IFFT result. O(N log N) vs O(N²) direct.
questions.correctAnswer: Time convolution = frequency multiplication. FFT both signals, multiply, IFFT result. O(N log N) vs O(N²) direct.
questions.explanation: Dry audio ∗ impulse response → FFT(audio)·FFT(IR) → IFFT. 10,000× faster for real-time convolution reverb.

questions.question: What is a spectrogram?
options: 2D visualization: time on x-axis, frequency on y-axis, color = magnitude. Generated by sliding FFT window.
questions.correctAnswer: 2D visualization: time on x-axis, frequency on y-axis, color = magnitude. Generated by sliding FFT window.
questions.explanation: Spectrogram = STFT (Short-Time Fourier Transform). Window function (Hann) reduces leakage. Hop size controls time resolution. Used in audio editors.

questions.e: Window functions prevent spectral leakage from the discontinuity at frame boundaries. Hann, Hamming, Blackman are common choices.
questions.explanation: The Hann window tapers the signal to zero at frame boundaries (raised cosine shape). Without it, the abrupt cut at frame edges creates spectral leakage — energy from one frequency bin bleeding into neighboring bins.
questions.question: What window function is commonly used before FFT and why?
options: Hann window — tapers signal to zero at edges, reducing spectral leakage from abrupt frame boundaries
questions.correctAnswer: Hann window — tapers signal to zero at edges, reducing spectral leakage from abrupt frame boundaries

questions.question: How does sound intensity fall off with distance?
options: Inverse-square law: I = P/(4πr²). Every distance doubling = -6 dB.
questions.correctAnswer: Inverse-square law: I = P/(4πr²). Every distance doubling = -6 dB.
questions.explanation: SPL(r) = SPL(1m) - 20·log₁₀(r). At 1m=80dB, 2m=74dB, 4m=68dB. Games clamp with min/max distance and configurable falloff.

questions.question: What cues does the brain use to localize sound?
questions.correctAnswer: ITD (interaural time difference), ILD (interaural level difference), and spectral notches (pinna filtering)
acceptableAnswers: time difference, level difference, pinna filtering
questions.explanation: ITD: ~0.25ms max. ILD: head shadow attenuates far ear (high freqs). Spectral notches: pinna shape → frequency dips that encode elevation.

questions.question: What is an HRTF?
options: Head-Related Transfer Function — pair of filters per direction capturing how sound reaches eardrums. Convolution applies spatialization.
questions.correctAnswer: Head-Related Transfer Function — pair of filters per direction capturing how sound reaches eardrums. Convolution applies spatialization.
questions.explanation: HRTF(θ,φ) = FIR filter (128-512 taps) per ear. Measured in anechoic chamber. Applied via convolution (FFT-based). Enables 3D audio on headphones.

questions.question: What causes the Doppler effect for sound?
options: Relative motion: f_heard = f_source·c/(c+v_relative). Approaching = higher pitch. Receding = lower.
questions.correctAnswer: Relative motion: f_heard = f_source·c/(c+v_relative). Approaching = higher pitch. Receding = lower.
questions.explanation: c = ~343 m/s. Car at 30 m/s approaching: f_heard = f_source·1.096 (~1.6 semitones higher). UE applies per-sound-source.

questions.e: Two speakers at center = +3dB if linear. Constant-power: -3dB each at center = combined 0dB. Total power constant across pan positions.
questions.explanation: Constant-power pan law: L = cos(θ+90°), R = sin(θ+90°). At center (θ=0): L=R=√2/2 ≈ 0.707 = -3dB each, but combined acoustic output = 0dB. Prevents the center phantom image from being louder than hard-panned sounds.
questions.question: What is constant-power pan law?
options: L=cos(θ+90°), R=sin(θ+90°). Prevents center from being +3dB louder than sides.
questions.correctAnswer: L=cos(θ+90°), R=sin(θ+90°). Prevents center from being +3dB louder than sides.

questions.question: Why is interpolation needed for networked multiplayer?
options: Server updates arrive at 10-20Hz (50-100ms apart). Client renders at 60Hz — must interpolate between received positions.
questions.correctAnswer: Server updates arrive at 10-20Hz (50-100ms apart). Client renders at 60Hz — must interpolate between received positions.
questions.explanation: Without interpolation: characters teleport between received positions. With: smooth motion, ~50ms render-behind latency (acceptable for other players).

questions.question: What is client-side prediction?
options: Client simulates own input locally for instant response, then reconciles with server's authoritative state
questions.correctAnswer: Client simulates own input locally for instant response, then reconciles with server's authoritative state
questions.explanation: Own character: can't wait 50ms RTT. Predict locally → send input → server corrects → rewind and replay. UE's CharacterMovementComponent does this.

questions.question: What is dead reckoning?
options: Extrapolating motion when packets stop arriving — assume constant velocity/acceleration
questions.correctAnswer: Extrapolating motion when packets stop arriving — assume constant velocity/acceleration
questions.explanation: Dead reckoning: last position + velocity·dt (+ ½·acceleration·dt²). After timeout (1-2s), freeze or disconnect. Used for packet loss.

questions.question: What is a jitter buffer?
options: Queue that delays packet playback to smooth network jitter — trades added latency for smooth motion
questions.correctAnswer: Queue that delays packet playback to smooth network jitter — trades added latency for smooth motion
questions.explanation: Packets arrive with variable delay. Buffer holds and releases at steady rate. Larger buffer = smoother + more latency. Adaptive buffers resize.

questions.e: Hermite: position + velocity at each endpoint. Produces smooth curves. Linear: constant velocity between updates, jerky direction changes.
questions.explanation: Hermite interpolation uses position AND velocity at both endpoints. This produces C¹-continuous curves — no sudden direction changes when a new server update arrives. Linear interpolation produces constant velocity between updates, causing visible jerks at each new packet.
questions.question: What is Hermite interpolation advantage over linear for networking?
options: Uses endpoint velocities for C¹ continuity — no sudden direction changes at received updates
questions.correctAnswer: Uses endpoint velocities for C¹ continuity — no sudden direction changes at received updates

questions.question: What does Geometric Algebra unify?
options: Vectors, complex numbers, quaternions, matrices, Plucker coordinates — all under one algebraic framework
questions.correctAnswer: Vectors, complex numbers, quaternions, matrices, Plucker coordinates — all under one algebraic framework
questions.explanation: GA: one algebra, one product (geometric product). Complex numbers = 2D even subalgebra. Quaternions = 3D even subalgebra. Unifies dot+cross.

questions.question: What is the geometric product of two vectors?
options: ab = a·b + a∧b — scalar (dot) PLUS bivector (wedge)
questions.correctAnswer: ab = a·b + a∧b — scalar (dot) PLUS bivector (wedge)
questions.explanation: Geometric product = symmetric part (dot, scalar) + antisymmetric part (wedge, bivector). Like complex numbers: real + imaginary.

questions.question: What are the grades in a 3D multivector?
options: Grade 0 (scalar), Grade 1 (vector, 3 components), Grade 2 (bivector, 3), Grade 3 (trivector, 1) — 2³=8 total
questions.correctAnswer: Grade 0 (scalar), Grade 1 (vector, 3 components), Grade 2 (bivector, 3), Grade 3 (trivector, 1) — 2³=8 total
questions.explanation: Pascal's triangle per dimension. 1,3,3,1 for 3D. Total = 2^dimension. Multivector = sum of all grades.

questions.question: Why is the cross product a 3D-only accident?
options: Cross product = Hodge dual of wedge: a×b = (a∧b)*. Only in 3D does the dual of a bivector give a vector. Wedge works in any dimension.
questions.correctAnswer: Cross product = Hodge dual of wedge: a×b = (a∧b)*. Only in 3D does the dual of a bivector give a vector. Wedge works in any dimension.
questions.explanation: In 3D: bivectors have 3 components = vectors have 3 components → duality possible. In 4D: bivectors have 6 components → can't map to 4-vector.

questions.e: 2D GA: basis {1, e₁, e₂, e₁₂}. Even subalgebra: {1, e₁₂}. e₁₂² = -1. This IS the complex numbers. i = e₁₂.
questions.explanation: In 3D, bivectors have 3 components (e₁₂, e₁₃, e₂₃) and vectors also have 3 components. Hodge dual maps between them: a×b = *(a∧b). In 4D, bivectors have 6 components — no dual to a 4-vector. The cross product only works in exactly 3 dimensions.
questions.question: How do complex numbers relate to GA?
options: Complex numbers ARE the even subalgebra of 2D GA — scalar + bivector. a+bi ↔ a + b·e₁₂.
questions.correctAnswer: Complex numbers ARE the even subalgebra of 2D GA — scalar + bivector. a+bi ↔ a + b·e₁₂.

questions.question: What is a rotor in Geometric Algebra?
options: The GA version of a quaternion — scalar + bivector. Rotates vectors via sandwich product: v' = RvR̃.
questions.correctAnswer: The GA version of a quaternion — scalar + bivector. Rotates vectors via sandwich product: v' = RvR̃.
