questions.explanation: Rotor: R = cos(θ/2) - sin(θ/2)B where B is unit bivector. Same 4 components as quaternion. Isomorphic to quaternions.

questions.question: What does the sandwich product RvR̃ do?
options: Rotates vector v by twice the angle encoded in rotor R. R̃ is reverse (conjugate) of R.
questions.correctAnswer: Rotates vector v by twice the angle encoded in rotor R. R̃ is reverse (conjugate) of R.
questions.explanation: Sandwich = universal GA pattern. Reflection: -nvn. Rotation: RvR̃ (two reflections). Translation: TvT̃. Everything is a sandwich.

questions.question: How are rotors more general than quaternions?
options: Rotors work in ANY dimension — 2D (complex numbers), 3D (quaternions), 4D+. Quaternions only in 3D.
questions.correctAnswer: Rotors work in ANY dimension — 2D (complex numbers), 3D (quaternions), 4D+. Quaternions only in 3D.
questions.explanation: Dimension-independence is the rotor's key advantage. 2D rotor = 2 components (complex). 3D rotor = 4 components (quaternion). 4D rotor = 8 components.

questions.e: Wedge product = 0 iff vectors are linearly dependent (parallel). a∧b = |a||b|sin(θ)·B. sin(0)=0, sin(180)=0.
questions.explanation: a∧b = 0 when vectors are linearly dependent — they point in the same or opposite direction. The parallelogram they span has zero area. sin(0)=sin(π)=0, so |a||b|sin(θ)=0.
questions.question: What does a∧b = 0 mean geometrically?
options: a and b are collinear (parallel) — they span zero area
questions.correctAnswer: a and b are collinear (parallel) — they span zero area

questions.e: I = Hodge dual operator. v·I converts vector to bivector. I²=-1 makes it behave like imaginary unit. Basis of complex/quaternion behavior in GA.
questions.explanation: I = e₁₂₃ is the unit volume element. I² = -1 makes it behave like the imaginary unit i. Multiplying a vector by I: v·I converts to bivector; multiplying a bivector by I: B·I converts to vector. This is the Hodge dual operation.
questions.question: What is the pseudoscalar I in 3D GA?
options: I = e₁₂₃ — the volume element. I² = -1 (in Euclidean). Multiplying by I flips grade (vector↔bivector, scalar↔trivector).
questions.correctAnswer: I = e₁₂₃ — the volume element. I² = -1 (in Euclidean). Multiplying by I flips grade (vector↔bivector, scalar↔trivector).

questions.question: What is Projective Geometric Algebra (PGA)?
options: Models 3D Euclidean geometry in a 4D algebra with degenerate metric (e₀²=0). Points=trivectors, lines=bivectors, planes=vectors.
questions.correctAnswer: Models 3D Euclidean geometry in a 4D algebra with degenerate metric (e₀²=0). Points=trivectors, lines=bivectors, planes=vectors.
questions.explanation: PGA: adds projective dimension. Points are intersections of 3 planes. Lines = join of 2 points. Ray-plane intersection = meet(L,Π). One operation.

questions.question: What is a motor in PGA?
options: Rigid body motion (rotation + translation) as one object. Motor = even-grade element. Acts via sandwich. Generalizes dual quaternions.
questions.correctAnswer: Rigid body motion (rotation + translation) as one object. Motor = even-grade element. Acts via sandwich. Generalizes dual quaternions.
questions.explanation: Motor slerp: one interpolation for rotation AND translation. No separate lerp(pos) + slerp(quat). Unified rigid body interpolation.

questions.question: How does PGA express ray-plane intersection?
options: L ∨ Π — the meet (regressive product) of a line L and plane Π is their intersection point. One algebraic product.
questions.correctAnswer: L ∨ Π — the meet (regressive product) of a line L and plane Π is their intersection point. One algebraic product.
questions.explanation: Join (∧) creates higher grade. Meet (∨) = intersection. Ray = join(camera, farPoint). Hit = meet(ray, plane). All algebra, no special cases.

questions.e: PGA = Cl(3,0,1) — degenerate metric. e₀²=0 enables translation via rotor-like sandwich. Same algebra models points, lines, planes, and rigid motions.
questions.explanation: e₀ is a null basis vector (e₀²=0). This degenerate metric is the mathematical trick that encodes translation in PGA. Null vectors represent ideal points at infinity; their exponential generates translations just as bivector exponentials generate rotations.
questions.question: Why is e₀² = 0 in PGA?
options: e₀ is a null vector representing the ideal point at origin — encodes translation. Null vectors are the key to modeling Euclidean geometry projectively.
questions.correctAnswer: e₀ is a null vector representing the ideal point at origin — encodes translation. Null vectors are the key to modeling Euclidean geometry projectively.

questions.e: Motor = even-grade element in PGA. 8 components: 1 scalar, 3 e₀ᵢ bivectors (translation), 3 eᵢⱼ bivectors (rotation), 1 e₀₁₂₃. Represents rigid body motion.
questions.explanation: A PGA motor has 8 components: 1 scalar + 3 e₀ᵢ bivectors (translation generators) + 3 eᵢⱼ bivectors (rotation generators) + 1 e₀₁₂₃ pseudoscalar. It is isomorphic to a dual quaternion and represents any rigid body motion.

questions.correctAnswer: 8 (scalar + 6 bivectors + pseudoscalar) — isomorphic to dual quaternion

questions.question: What makes A* different from Dijkstra's algorithm?
options: A* uses a heuristic h(n) estimating distance to goal — guides search toward target. Dijkstra is h(n)=0 (explores all directions).
questions.correctAnswer: A* uses a heuristic h(n) estimating distance to goal — guides search toward target. Dijkstra is h(n)=0 (explores all directions).
questions.explanation: f(n) = g(n) + h(n). g = cost from start. h = estimated cost to goal. If h is admissible (never overestimates), A* finds optimal path.

questions.question: What makes a heuristic admissible for A*?
options: h(n) must never overestimate the true remaining cost — typically Euclidean distance for spatial grids
questions.correctAnswer: h(n) must never overestimate the true remaining cost — typically Euclidean distance for spatial grids
questions.explanation: Admissible = optimistic. Euclidean distance ≤ actual path distance. Manhattan distance on grid = admissible (but not on diagonals).

questions.question: What data structure makes A* efficient?
options: Priority queue (min-heap) — always expand the node with lowest f(n) first
questions.correctAnswer: Priority queue (min-heap) — always expand the node with lowest f(n) first
questions.explanation: Open set = min-heap by f(n). Extract-min gives best candidate. Closed set = visited nodes. O(b^d) worst case; heuristic reduces this dramatically.

questions.e: Navmesh: convex polygons = nodes. Shared edges = connections. Much fewer nodes than grid cells. UE generates navmesh from level geometry.
questions.explanation: Navmesh drastically reduces search space compared to grids — dozens of convex polygons vs thousands of grid cells. A* on navmesh searches polygon connectivity graph. UE generates navmeshes using Recast.
questions.question: What is a navigation mesh (navmesh)?
options: A polygon mesh representing walkable areas — A* finds paths along mesh edges/vertices, much fewer nodes than grids
questions.correctAnswer: A polygon mesh representing walkable areas — A* finds paths along mesh edges/vertices, much fewer nodes than grids

questions.e: Navmesh drastically reduces search space. UE's Recast/Detour generates navmeshes. Pathfinding on navmesh = string-pulling for smooth final path.
questions.explanation: Dijkstra = A* with h(n)=0. Without heuristic guidance, Dijkstra explores equally in all directions. A* uses Euclidean/Manhattan distance to guide search toward the goal — dramatically fewer expanded nodes.
questions.question: What is the difference between A* on a grid vs a navmesh?
options: Grid: thousands of cells, Manhattan/Euclidean heuristic. Navmesh: dozens of polygons, Euclidean heuristic between centroids + edge costs. Navmesh is much faster.
questions.correctAnswer: Grid: thousands of cells, Manhattan/Euclidean heuristic. Navmesh: dozens of polygons, Euclidean heuristic between centroids + edge costs. Navmesh is much faster.

questions.question: What are Craig Reynolds' steering behaviors?
options: Simple forces combining to produce complex motion: seek, flee, arrive, wander, separation, alignment, cohesion
questions.correctAnswer: Simple forces combining to produce complex motion: seek, flee, arrive, wander, separation, alignment, cohesion
questions.explanation: Steering = desired velocity - current velocity. Each behavior computes a force. Combine via weighted sum or priority. Emergent flocking from 3 rules.

questions.question: What three rules produce flocking/boids behavior?
questions.correctAnswer: Separation (avoid neighbors), alignment (match average heading), cohesion (move toward center of neighbors)
questions.explanation: Reynolds 1987. Three simple rules = emergent flock. No leader, no centralized control. Each boid reacts only to local neighbors.

questions.question: What is the arrive behavior?
options: Seek with deceleration — slows down as it approaches target, stops at target instead of overshooting
questions.correctAnswer: Seek with deceleration — slows down as it approaches target, stops at target instead of overshooting
questions.explanation: Arrive: if within slowing radius, scale speed by distance/radius. Seek would overshoot and orbit. Arrive smoothly decelerates to zero.

questions.e: For each neighbor within radius: compute repulsion vector away from neighbor. Sum and normalize. Key component of flocking.
questions.explanation: Separation steers away from nearby neighbors within a defined radius. Force is inversely proportional to distance — closer neighbors push harder. Sum repulsion vectors, normalize. Prevents agents from crowding or overlapping.
questions.question: What is separation steering?
options: Steer away from nearby neighbors — prevents crowding. Force inversely proportional to distance.
questions.correctAnswer: Steer away from nearby neighbors — prevents crowding. Force inversely proportional to distance.

questions.e: Wander: circle projected ahead of agent. Random displacement on circle changes each frame. Produces natural-looking random movement.
questions.explanation: Wander projects a circle ahead of the agent and picks a random point on that circle each frame. The agent steers toward that point, producing smooth random-looking movement. The circle radius and distance control turning rate and randomness.
questions.question: What is wander steering?
options: Produces random, smooth wandering motion — projects a circle ahead, picks random point on circle, steers toward it
questions.correctAnswer: Produces random, smooth wandering motion — projects a circle ahead, picks random point on circle, steers toward it

questions.question: What algorithm generates navigation meshes in UE?

questions.question: What is 'string pulling' in pathfinding?
options: Post-processing step that smooths the A* path on a navmesh — removes unnecessary waypoints by pulling the path tight
questions.correctAnswer: Post-processing step that smooths the A* path on a navmesh — removes unnecessary waypoints by pulling the path tight
questions.explanation: A* on navmesh gives polygon corners. String pulling (funnel algorithm) smooths to shortest path within the navmesh corridor.

questions.e: Detour = runtime component. Uses Recast-generated data. Provides pathfinding queries, local avoidance, crowd movement.
questions.explanation: Recast step 1 rasterizes input triangles into a voxel heightfield. Each column stores spans of solid cells. A span is walkable if the surface slope < maxSlope (typically 45°) and the step height to adjacent spans < maxStep (agent can climb).
questions.question: What does Detour do at runtime?
options: Pathfinding queries on the pre-generated navmesh — A*, string pulling, crowd simulation
questions.correctAnswer: Pathfinding queries on the pre-generated navmesh — A*, string pulling, crowd simulation

questions.e: Recast: triangle mesh → voxel heightfield. Each column stores walkable spans. Filter by agent height, max slope, max step.
questions.explanation: After A* finds a sequence of navmesh polygons, the funnel algorithm tightens the path to the shortest route within that polygon corridor. It's like pulling a string taut through the polygons — removes unnecessary detours at polygon corners.
questions.question: What is the voxelization step in Recast?
options: Converts 3D geometry into a heightfield — voxels marked as walkable if slope < max and step height < max
questions.correctAnswer: Converts 3D geometry into a heightfield — voxels marked as walkable if slope < max and step height < max

questions.e: Recast step 1: rasterize input geometry into heightfield. Compact representation: spans per column. Filter by walkability parameters.
questions.explanation: Detour is the runtime component that uses Recast-generated navmesh data. It provides pathfinding queries (A* on polygon graph), crowd simulation (local avoidance), and dynamic obstacle handling. Recast = offline generation; Detour = runtime queries.
questions.question: What is the Recast voxelization process?
options: Converts triangle mesh to 3D voxel grid. Each column stores spans of solid cells. Walkable if slope < maxSlope and step height < maxStep.
questions.correctAnswer: Converts triangle mesh to 3D voxel grid. Each column stores spans of solid cells. Walkable if slope < maxSlope and step height < maxStep.

questions.e: A* gives polygon sequence. Funnel algorithm tightens to shortest path staying within the corridor. Produces natural-looking smooth paths.
questions.explanation: Recast converts triangle soup to voxel heightfield → filters walkable surfaces → partitions into contiguous regions → extracts region contours → triangulates into convex polygons → adds height detail for precise placement. Six-stage pipeline.

questions.question: What is the two-phase collision detection architecture?
options: Broad phase (cheap, find potential pairs) + Narrow phase (expensive, precise test on candidates)
questions.correctAnswer: Broad phase (cheap, find potential pairs) + Narrow phase (expensive, precise test on candidates)
questions.explanation: Broad phase: O(n log n) spatial partitioning (grid, octree, sweep-and-prune). Narrow phase: geometric tests (AABB, SAT, GJK).

questions.question: What is the tunneling problem?
options: Fast-moving object passes through thin wall in one frame — collision missed between discrete checks
questions.correctAnswer: Fast-moving object passes through thin wall in one frame — collision missed between discrete checks
questions.explanation: Solution: CCD (Continuous Collision Detection) — sweep shape along path. Or sub-stepping (run physics at higher frequency). UE: bUseCCD.

questions.e: Sweep-and-prune (sort and sweep): O(n log n) sort, then linear sweep. Good for many moving objects. Used in Bullet Physics.
questions.explanation: DBVT = Dynamic Bounding Volume Tree. Nodes are AABBs that can be updated incrementally when objects move — much cheaper than rebuilding. UE5 Chaos physics uses this internally.
questions.question: What is the sweep-and-prune broad phase?
options: Sort AABB min-x values, sweep across. Objects with overlapping x-intervals are candidates. Extend to y, z.
questions.correctAnswer: Sort AABB min-x values, sweep across. Objects with overlapping x-intervals are candidates. Extend to y, z.

questions.e: Discrete = snapshot at t=n and t=n+1. CCD = swept volume between. Bullet traveling 1000m/s moves 41m per frame — discrete misses walls.
questions.explanation: Broad phase: uniform grids (many similar objects), octrees (uneven distribution), DBVT (dynamic worlds), sweep-and-prune (sorted AABB min-x). All reduce O(n²) all-pairs to ~O(n log n).
questions.question: What is the difference between discrete and continuous collision detection?
options: Discrete: checks positions at frame boundaries (can miss fast objects). CCD: sweeps shape along path, catches tunneling.
questions.correctAnswer: Discrete: checks positions at frame boundaries (can miss fast objects). CCD: sweeps shape along path, catches tunneling.
Uniform grids, quadtrees/octrees, dynamic AABB trees (DBVT), and sweep-and-prune
acceptableAnswers: grids, octrees, DBVT, sweep and prune
uniform grid, quadtree, octree, DBVT, sort and sweep
questions.explanation: Broad phase reduces O(n²) to O(n log n). Grid: many similar-sized objects. Octree: uneven distribution. DBVT: dynamic worlds (UE uses this).
questions.question: What spatial partitioning structures are used for broad phase?

questions.e: DBVT = dynamic bounding volume tree. Leaves are object AABBs. Rotated/translated nodes update cheaply. UE5 Chaos physics uses this.
questions.explanation: Discrete CC checks positions at frame start and end — a bullet traveling 1000m/s moves 41m per 24fps frame and can pass through walls. CCD sweeps the shape's volume along its trajectory.
questions.question: What is a dynamic AABB tree (DBVT)?
options: Binary tree of AABBs — nodes can move incrementally updated. UE's internal broad phase structure.
questions.correctAnswer: Binary tree of AABBs — nodes can move incrementally updated. UE's internal broad phase structure.

questions.question: How do you test if two AABBs overlap?
options: Check if intervals overlap on ALL three axes: (a_min ≤ b_max) AND (a_max ≥ b_min) for x, y, z
questions.correctAnswer: Check if intervals overlap on ALL three axes: (a_min ≤ b_max) AND (a_max ≥ b_min) for x, y, z
questions.explanation: AABB-AABB = 6 comparisons (2 per axis). O(1). Separating axis: if any axis has gap, no overlap. All 3 overlap = collision.

questions.question: How do you find the closest point on an AABB to a point?
options: Clamp each coordinate: x = clamp(px, min.x, max.x), same for y, z
questions.correctAnswer: Clamp each coordinate: x = clamp(px, min.x, max.x), same for y, z
questions.explanation: Clamp to each axis-aligned interval. Result is the point ON or INSIDE the AABB closest to the query point. Basis of sphere-AABB test.

questions.e: |c1 - c2| < r1 + r2. Square both sides to avoid sqrt: |c1-c2|² < (r1+r2)². Fastest collision test. Use for broad-phase rough checks.
questions.explanation: AABB-AABB intersection tests if intervals overlap on all three axes: a_min ≤ b_max AND a_max ≥ b_min for X, Y, Z. Only 6 comparisons total. O(1). This is the fastest general collision test.
questions.question: How do you test sphere-sphere collision?
options: Distance between centers < sum of radii. O(1), one distance calculation.
questions.correctAnswer: Distance between centers < sum of radii. O(1), one distance calculation.
Find closest point on AABB to sphere center (clamp each coordinate), then check distance to that point < sphere radius
acceptableAnswers: closest point on AABB then distance check
questions.explanation: 1. Clamp sphere center to AABB min/max → closest point. 2. Distance from closest point to center < radius = collision. O(1).
questions.question: How do you test sphere-AABB collision?

questions.e: AABB = cheap, fast, recompute on rotation. OBB = rotates with object, tighter fit, more expensive. Broad phase uses AABBs.
questions.explanation: Spheres are rotationally invariant — the test doesn't change when the sphere rotates. AABBs must be recomputed when an object rotates (the bounding box gets larger). Spheres are simpler but less tight for elongated shapes.
questions.question: What is the advantage of AABBs over OBBs?
options: AABB: axis-aligned — intersection test is 6 comparisons (O(1)). OBB: oriented — SAT needs 15 axes. AABB much faster, less precise.
questions.correctAnswer: AABB: axis-aligned — intersection test is 6 comparisons (O(1)). OBB: oriented — SAT needs 15 axes. AABB much faster, less precise.

questions.e: |c1-c2|² < (r1+r2)². No SQRT needed. 3 subtractions + 3 multiplies + 2 additions + 1 compare. ~7 operations total.
questions.explanation: AABBs are always axis-aligned — must be recomputed when objects rotate, and the new AABB may be much larger than the object (a rotated sword gets a huge box). OBBs rotate with the object for a tighter fit but cost more to test.
questions.question: How does sphere-sphere collision work?
options: Distance between centers < sum of radii. Square both sides to avoid sqrt. O(1), fastest collision test.
questions.correctAnswer: Distance between centers < sum of radii. Square both sides to avoid sqrt. O(1), fastest collision test.

questions.question: What is the slab method for ray-AABB intersection?
options: Compute ray entry/exit times for each axis pair. Overlap of all intervals = hit. t_entry ≤ t_exit for all 3 axes.
questions.correctAnswer: Compute ray entry/exit times for each axis pair. Overlap of all intervals = hit. t_entry ≤ t_exit for all 3 axes.
questions.explanation: For each axis: t_near = (min - origin) / dir, t_far = (max - origin) / dir. t_enter = max of t_nears. t_exit = min of t_fars. Hit if t_enter ≤ t_exit.

questions.question: What is the Moller-Trumbore algorithm?
options: Fast ray-triangle intersection — solves barycentric coordinates via Cramer's rule with precomputed edge vectors. O(1).
questions.correctAnswer: Fast ray-triangle intersection — solves barycentric coordinates via Cramer's rule with precomputed edge vectors. O(1).
questions.explanation: M-T: computes (u,v,t) where u,v are barycentrics and t is ray distance. Hit if u≥0, v≥0, u+v≤1, t>0. Standard method for ray-triangle.

questions.e: For each axis: t_near = (min - origin)/dir, t_far = (max - origin)/dir. Overlap of all 3 intervals = hit. Handle division by zero (parallel rays).
questions.explanation: The slab method computes t_near = (min - origin)/dir and t_far = (max - origin)/dir for each axis. t_enter = max of t_nears, t_exit = min of t_fars. Intersection if t_enter ≤ t_exit and t_exit ≥ 0. Handle parallel rays (dir=0) by checking if origin is between min and max.
questions.question: What is the slab method for ray-AABB intersection?
options: Compute ray entry/exit times for each axis pair. t_enter = max(t_nears), t_exit = min(t_fars). Hit if t_enter ≤ t_exit.
questions.correctAnswer: Compute ray entry/exit times for each axis pair. t_enter = max(t_nears), t_exit = min(t_fars). Hit if t_enter ≤ t_exit.

questions.e: M-T: precomputes edge vectors. Solves (u,v,t) where u,v are barycentrics. Hit if u≥0, v≥0, u+v≤1, t>0. Industry standard for ray-triangle.
questions.explanation: Möller-Trumbore solves ray-triangle via Cramer's rule with precomputed edge vectors E1=P1-P0, E2=P2-P0. Computes barycentric coordinates (u,v) and ray distance t. Intersection if u≥0, v≥0, u+v≤1, t≥0. Standard in every ray tracer and GPU.
questions.question: What is the Moller-Trumbore algorithm?
options: Fast ray-triangle intersection — solves barycentric coordinates via Cramer's rule. O(1). Standard method.
questions.correctAnswer: Fast ray-triangle intersection — solves barycentric coordinates via Cramer's rule. O(1). Standard method.

questions.e: Parametric form: one parameter t. Each t gives a point. t < 0 is behind the ray (usually ignored). Direction should be normalized.
questions.explanation: P(t) = Origin + t·Direction with t≥0. At t=0: ray origin. t increases: points further along ray. t<0: behind the ray (usually discarded). Direction should be normalized for meaningful t values (t = distance along ray).
questions.question: What is the parametric ray equation?
options: P(t) = Origin + t·Direction, t≥0. t=0 at origin, t increases along ray.
questions.correctAnswer: P(t) = Origin + t·Direction, t≥0. t=0 at origin, t increases along ray.

questions.question: What is the Separating Axis Theorem (SAT)?
options: Two convex polyhedra don't overlap iff there exists an axis where their projections don't overlap. Test candidate axes.
questions.correctAnswer: Two convex polyhedra don't overlap iff there exists an axis where their projections don't overlap. Test candidate axes.
questions.explanation: SAT: project both shapes onto axis. If projections don't overlap → separating axis found → NO collision. If ALL candidate axes overlap → collision.

questions.question: Which axes must be tested for OBB-OBB SAT?
questions.correctAnswer: 15 axes: 3 face normals of A + 3 face normals of B + 9 cross products of edges (3×3)
acceptableAnswers: face normals plus edge cross products
questions.explanation: Box A: 3 face normals. Box B: 3 face normals. Cross products: 3 edges of A × 3 edges of B = 9 axes. Total: 15. Test all 15.

questions.e: SAT is exact for convex polyhedra. Number of axes tested depends on shape complexity. OBB-OBB = 15 axes. Triangle-triangle = 11 axes.
questions.explanation: OBB-OBB tests 15 axes: 3 face normals of A + 3 face normals of B + 9 cross products (each edge of A × each edge of B). The cross product axes catch cases where neither face normal separates — two boxes nearly touching at corners.
questions.question: What shape pairs can SAT handle?
options: Any convex polyhedra — works for OBB, convex hulls, capsules. Requires enumerating candidate separating axes.
questions.correctAnswer: Any convex polyhedra — works for OBB, convex hulls, capsules. Requires enumerating candidate separating axes.

questions.e: SAT: fixed axis set, O(num_faces²) for OBB. GJK: iterative, ~3-6 iterations for convex shapes. GJK is more general.
questions.explanation: SAT is exact for convex polyhedra — you enumerate a finite set of candidate axes, and if ANY separates, there is no collision. For general convex shapes, SAT works but the axis set depends on the shape complexity.
questions.question: What is the difference between SAT and GJK?
options: SAT tests static set of axes (good for boxes). GJK iteratively finds separating axis (works for ANY convex shape, no pre-computed axes).
questions.correctAnswer: SAT tests static set of axes (good for boxes). GJK iteratively finds separating axis (works for ANY convex shape, no pre-computed axes).

questions.e: Edge-edge cross products catch cases where neither face normal separates. Example: two boxes almost touching at corners, no face normal separates.
questions.explanation: Project both shapes onto the candidate axis: interval = [min(vertices·axis), max(vertices·axis)]. If the two intervals don't overlap, this axis separates the shapes → NO collision. If ALL candidate axes have overlapping intervals → collision.
questions.question: Why test cross products of edges for OBB SAT?
options: Two boxes can have a separating axis that is perpendicular to an edge from each — the cross product direction. 3×3=9 additional axes.
questions.correctAnswer: Two boxes can have a separating axis that is perpendicular to an edge from each — the cross product direction. 3×3=9 additional axes.

questions.question: What does GJK compute?
options: Whether two convex shapes overlap — by checking if the origin is inside their Minkowski difference
questions.correctAnswer: Whether two convex shapes overlap — by checking if the origin is inside their Minkowski difference
questions.explanation: GJK: iteratively builds simplex in Minkowski difference space (A - B). If origin is inside simplex → overlap. Support function finds extreme points.

questions.question: What is the Minkowski difference used in GJK?
options: A - B = {a - b | a∈A, b∈B}. If origin ∈ (A - B), then A and B intersect.
questions.correctAnswer: A - B = {a - b | a∈A, b∈B}. If origin ∈ (A - B), then A and B intersect.
questions.explanation: Minkowski difference transforms intersection test into origin-inclusion test. GJK doesn't need to explicitly compute A - B — uses support function.

questions.question: What is the support function in GJK?
options: Returns the point in a shape furthest in a given direction — used to build the simplex iteratively
questions.correctAnswer: Returns the point in a shape furthest in a given direction — used to build the simplex iteratively
questions.explanation: support(shape, direction) = argmax(p·direction for p in shape). GJK calls this to find next simplex vertex. O(1) for simple shapes, O(n) for meshes.

questions.e: GJK detects overlap. EPA computes MTV (Minimum Translation Vector) — how far and in what direction to push shapes apart. GJK+EPA = full collision response.
questions.explanation: EPA (Expanding Polytope Algorithm) runs after GJK detects overlap. It expands the simplex outward until it touches the Minkowski difference boundary, computing the Minimum Translation Vector — the shortest distance and direction to separate the shapes.
questions.question: What is EPA (Expanding Polytope Algorithm)?
options: Extends GJK — after detecting overlap, EPA expands simplex to find penetration depth and contact normal
questions.correctAnswer: Extends GJK — after detecting overlap, EPA expands simplex to find penetration depth and contact normal

questions.e: Minkowski difference shrinks A to a point and expands B by A's shape. If that expanded shape contains the origin, they overlap.
questions.explanation: A - B = {a-b | a∈A, b∈B}. This transforms the intersection test into an origin-inclusion test: if the origin is inside A-B, then A and B share at least one point (intersect). GJK tests origin inclusion without explicitly computing A-B.
questions.question: What is the Minkowski difference?
options: A - B = {a-b | a∈A, b∈B}. If origin ∈ (A-B), A and B intersect. Transforms intersection to origin test.
questions.correctAnswer: A - B = {a-b | a∈A, b∈B}. If origin ∈ (A-B), A and B intersect. Transforms intersection to origin test.

questions.e: support(shape, d) = extreme point in direction d. O(1) for sphere/box. O(n) for convex hull. GJK never needs explicit Minkowski difference.
questions.explanation: The support function returns the point in a shape furthest in a given direction: argmax(p·d for p in shape). For a sphere: center + radius·d̂. For a box: corner with signs matching direction. GJK uses support functions to build the simplex iteratively.
questions.question: What is the support function in GJK?
options: Returns point in shape furthest in given direction: argmax(p·d for p in shape). GJK calls this to build simplex.
questions.correctAnswer: Returns point in shape furthest in given direction: argmax(p·d for p in shape). GJK calls this to build simplex.

questions.question: What does Newton's Second Law (F=ma) govern in physics engines?
options: Linear acceleration: a = F/m. Integrate acceleration to get velocity, velocity to get position.
questions.correctAnswer: Linear acceleration: a = F/m. Integrate acceleration to get velocity, velocity to get position.
questions.explanation: Forces cause acceleration. Gravity: F=mg. Friction, springs, constraints all produce forces. Integration accumulates force effects over time.

questions.question: What is the inertia tensor?
options: 3×3 matrix describing how mass is distributed — determines angular acceleration from torque: τ = I·α
questions.correctAnswer: 3×3 matrix describing how mass is distributed — determines angular acceleration from torque: τ = I·α
questions.explanation: Rotational analog of mass. Inertia tensor depends on shape and mass distribution. Diagonalized in principal axes. Harder to rotate around axes with larger inertia.

questions.e: ω is a 3D vector. Direction = axis of rotation (right-hand rule). magnitude = radians per second. L = I·ω (angular momentum).
questions.explanation: The inertia tensor I is a 3×3 matrix describing mass distribution. The same torque applied around different axes produces different angular acceleration depending on how mass is distributed. τ = I·α (rotational Newton's Second Law).
questions.question: What is angular velocity?
options: ω — rate of rotation around an axis. Direction = rotation axis, magnitude = speed (rad/s).
questions.correctAnswer: ω — rate of rotation around an axis. Direction = rotation axis, magnitude = speed (rad/s).

questions.e: Rotational mass. Sphere: diagonal (all equal). Box: diagonal (different per axis). Non-diagonal for arbitrary shapes. Diagonalized in body frame.
questions.explanation: 6 DOF: position(3) + orientation(3). Orientation can be Euler angles (3), quaternion (4 with 1 constraint), or rotation matrix (9 with 6 constraints). State vector includes: position, velocity, orientation, angular velocity.
questions.question: What is the inertia tensor?
options: 3×3 matrix describing mass distribution. τ = I·α (torque = inertia tensor × angular acceleration). Depends on shape.
questions.correctAnswer: 3×3 matrix describing mass distribution. τ = I·α (torque = inertia tensor × angular acceleration). Depends on shape.
3 translational (x, y, z) + 3 rotational (pitch, yaw, roll)
acceptableAnswers: x y z pitch yaw roll

questions.e: COM = weighted average of mass distribution. Linear dynamics use COM position and velocity. Rotation is around COM.
questions.explanation: Center of mass is the weighted average of mass distribution. Linear dynamics (F=ma) apply at COM. Forces applied away from COM create torque (τ = r × F) and cause rotation. COM simplifies: translate like a point mass, rotate around COM.
questions.question: What is the center of mass?
options: The point where all mass is concentrated for linear motion. Forces applied off-center create torque (rotation).
questions.correctAnswer: The point where all mass is concentrated for linear motion. Forces applied off-center create torque (rotation).

questions.question: What is Euler integration?
options: Simplest: pos += vel·dt; vel += acc·dt. O(dt) error per step. Unstable for stiff systems.
questions.correctAnswer: Simplest: pos += vel·dt; vel += acc·dt. O(dt) error per step. Unstable for stiff systems.
questions.explanation: Explicit Euler: first-order. Cheap but diverges for oscillatory systems unless dt is very small. Game physics at 60Hz often uses semi-implicit Euler.

questions.question: What is semi-implicit Euler (symplectic)?
options: vel += acc·dt FIRST, then pos += vel·dt. More stable than explicit Euler for same cost. Standard in game physics.
questions.correctAnswer: vel += acc·dt FIRST, then pos += vel·dt. More stable than explicit Euler for same cost. Standard in game physics.
questions.explanation: Symplectic Euler preserves energy better than explicit. Order matters: update velocity first, then use NEW velocity for position. UE physics uses this.

questions.e: Verlet: no explicit velocity. Velocity = (pos(t+dt)-pos(t-dt))/(2·dt). Used in molecular dynamics, cloth simulation.
questions.explanation: Explicit Euler: v(t+dt) = v(t) + a(t)·dt using acceleration from START of step. The error accumulates, adding energy to the system — orbits spiral outward, springs oscillate with growing amplitude. Semi-implicit uses v(t+dt) for position update, improving stability.
questions.question: What is Verlet integration?
options: pos(t+dt)=2·pos(t)-pos(t-dt)+acc·dt². Velocity-free, time-reversible, good energy conservation.
questions.correctAnswer: pos(t+dt)=2·pos(t)-pos(t-dt)+acc·dt². Velocity-free, time-reversible, good energy conservation.

questions.e: Fixed timestep = stability, reproducibility. Variable = frame-synced. UE physics runs at fixed rate (default 60Hz) with accumulator for sub-steps.
questions.explanation: RK4 evaluates the derivative at 4 points within a step and takes a weighted average. This gives O(dt⁵) error per step — much more accurate than Euler's O(dt²). Used where accuracy matters more than speed (cinematic physics, scientific simulation).
questions.question: What is fixed vs variable timestep in physics?
options: Fixed: deterministic, reproducible. Variable: frame-rate matched. UE: fixed physics timestep with sub-stepping for stability.
questions.correctAnswer: Fixed: deterministic, reproducible. Variable: frame-rate matched. UE: fixed physics timestep with sub-stepping for stability.

questions.e: Explicit Euler evaluates derivative at START of step. Semi-implicit uses updated velocity for position — much better energy behavior.
questions.explanation: F = -k·x - c·v. Spring force proportional to displacement (-k·x, Hooke's law). Damping force proportional to velocity (-c·v). Stiff springs (large k) require tiny dt for explicit methods to remain stable.
questions.question: Why does explicit Euler gain energy?
options: Each step slightly overshoots the true trajectory — error accumulates, orbits expand, springs explode. Semi-implicit fixes this.
questions.correctAnswer: Each step slightly overshoots the true trajectory — error accumulates, orbits expand, springs explode. Semi-implicit fixes this.

questions.e: RK4: 4 evaluations per step. k1=f(t,y), k2=f(t+dt/2,y+k1·dt/2), k3, k4. Average with weights [1,2,2,1]. Used where accuracy > speed.
questions.explanation: Verlet: x(t+dt) = 2x(t) - x(t-dt) + a(t)·dt². No explicit velocity storage. Velocity derived as (x(t+dt)-x(t-dt))/(2dt). Time-reversible and excellent energy conservation — standard for molecular dynamics and cloth simulation.
questions.question: What is RK4 integration?
options: 4th-order Runge-Kutta: evaluates derivative at 4 points per step, weighted average. O(dt⁵) error. Expensive but very accurate.
questions.correctAnswer: 4th-order Runge-Kutta: evaluates derivative at 4 points per step, weighted average. O(dt⁵) error. Expensive but very accurate.

questions.e: Hooke's law + damping. k=stiffness, c=damping. Stiff springs (large k) require tiny dt for explicit methods. Implicit methods handle stiff springs better.
questions.explanation: Fixed timestep = deterministic, reproducible physics. Variable = frame-rate matched but non-deterministic. UE runs physics at a fixed rate (default 60Hz) using an accumulator — renders at variable FPS, steps physics at fixed intervals with sub-stepping.
questions.question: What is the spring-mass-damper system?
options: F = -k·x - c·v. Spring force proportional to displacement, damping proportional to velocity. Stiff springs need small dt.
questions.correctAnswer: F = -k·x - c·v. Spring force proportional to displacement, damping proportional to velocity. Stiff springs need small dt.

questions.question: What is a constraint in physics simulation?
options: A rule enforcing a relationship between bodies — distance joint, hinge, contact (non-penetration). Solved iteratively.
questions.correctAnswer: A rule enforcing a relationship between bodies — distance joint, hinge, contact (non-penetration). Solved iteratively.
questions.explanation: Constraints: equality (joints) and inequality (contacts). Solved via sequential impulse (projected Gauss-Seidel) in most game engines.

questions.e: Projected Gauss-Seidel. Solve each constraint independently, repeat. Converges over iterations. UE's PhysX/Chaos use 4-8 iterations per frame.
questions.explanation: A Lagrange multiplier λ is the force magnitude needed to enforce a constraint. Each constraint equation C(x)=0 produces one scalar λ. The solver finds the λ values that satisfy all constraints simultaneously via the linear system J·v + b = 0.
questions.question: What is sequential impulse solving?
options: Iteratively resolves constraints one at a time — projects velocities to satisfy constraint, repeats. Standard in game physics.
questions.correctAnswer: Iteratively resolves constraints one at a time — projects velocities to satisfy constraint, repeats. Standard in game physics.

questions.e: λ = constraint force. J·v + b = 0 is the velocity-level constraint. Solver computes λ that satisfies all constraints.
questions.explanation: Baumgarte stabilization adds a position correction term: b = (β/dt)·C(x) where β≈0.1-0.3. This proportional feedback drives position errors toward zero over multiple timesteps. Too high β causes jitter; too low allows visible penetration.
questions.question: What is a Lagrange multiplier in constraint solving?
options: The force magnitude needed to enforce a constraint. Each constraint equation produces one λ. Solver finds all λ simultaneously.
questions.correctAnswer: The force magnitude needed to enforce a constraint. Each constraint equation produces one λ. Solver finds all λ simultaneously.

questions.e: Baumgarte: b = (β/dt)·C(x). β = 0.1-0.3. Corrects position errors gradually. Too high = jitter; too low = visible penetration.
questions.explanation: C(x) = |p₁-p₂| - L = 0. The Jacobian J is the direction vector between the two points. When distance = rest length L, constraint is satisfied. When |p₁-p₂| > L, constraint is violated and solver applies corrective impulses along the connection direction.
questions.question: What is Baumgarte stabilization?
options: Adds position error correction to velocity constraint: bias term proportional to penetration depth. Prevents drift.
questions.correctAnswer: Adds position error correction to velocity constraint: bias term proportional to penetration depth. Prevents drift.

questions.e: C(x) = |p1-p2| - L. Jacobian J = direction vector. Satisfied when distance = rest length. Violation = stretch/compression.
questions.explanation: Sequential impulse (Projected Gauss-Seidel) solves constraints one at a time: compute impulse for constraint i, apply velocity update, move to constraint i+1. Repeated iterations converge to the global solution. PhysX uses ~4-10 iterations.
questions.question: What is a distance constraint?
options: |p1 - p2| = restLength. Maintains fixed distance between two points. Used for ropes, ragdoll joints.
questions.correctAnswer: |p1 - p2| = restLength. Maintains fixed distance between two points. Used for ropes, ragdoll joints.

questions.question: What is Time of Impact (TOI)?
options: The exact time two moving shapes first touch. CCD computes TOI to prevent tunneling for fast-moving objects.
questions.correctAnswer: The exact time two moving shapes first touch. CCD computes TOI to prevent tunneling for fast-moving objects.
questions.explanation: TOI = t ∈ [0,1] where first contact occurs. Conservative advancement: iterative search for TOI by advancing shapes until distance < epsilon.

questions.e: Conservative advancement: move by distance to closest feature (safe step). Compute new closest distance. Repeat until distance < epsilon. Bullet, PhysX use this.
questions.explanation: TOI ∈ [0,1] is the exact time within the timestep when two moving shapes first contact. CCD sweeps shapes along their trajectories to compute TOI. The result positions objects exactly at the point of contact, preventing tunneling.
questions.question: What is conservative advancement for TOI?
options: Iterative search: advance shapes by safe distance (guaranteed no collision), check again. Converges to TOI.
questions.correctAnswer: Iterative search: advance shapes by safe distance (guaranteed no collision), check again. Converges to TOI.

questions.e: TOI ∈ [0,1]. Conservative advancement: iteratively shrink time interval until contact. TOI=0.5 means collision at midpoint of timestep.
questions.explanation: Conservative advancement iteratively narrows the TOI interval: compute closest distance d between shapes, advance time by d/(relative speed) — guaranteed no collision in this sub-interval. Repeat until distance < epsilon. Converges to exact TOI.
questions.question: What is time of impact (TOI)?
options: The exact time within a timestep when two moving shapes first touch. CCD computes TOI to position objects at contact.
questions.correctAnswer: The exact time within a timestep when two moving shapes first touch. CCD computes TOI to position objects at contact.

questions.e: CA: advance shapes by d/(max speed). Recompute distance. Repeat until d < epsilon. Converges to TOI. Used by PhysX, Bullet.
questions.explanation: CCD sweeps actual shape geometry precisely (expensive, accurate). Speculative contacts expand contact tolerance by velocity·dt — effectively inflating AABBs to catch fast objects. Much cheaper but can produce false positives. UE5 Chaos uses speculative contacts by default.
questions.question: What is conservative advancement?
options: Iterative TOI search: compute closest distance d, advance by d/(relative speed) — guaranteed no collision this step, repeat.
questions.correctAnswer: Iterative TOI search: compute closest distance d, advance by d/(relative speed) — guaranteed no collision this step, repeat.

questions.e: UE5 Chaos: speculative contacts = inflated AABB by velocity*dt. Cheaper than CCD. Good for most fast objects. CCD for critical cases (bullets).
questions.explanation: Bullet-through-paper: a bullet at 1000 m/s travels 41m between 24fps frames. Discrete checks at frame boundaries miss the 1cm wall entirely. CCD sweeps the bullet's path as a continuous volume and detects the intermediate collision.
questions.question: What is the difference between CCD and speculative contacts?
options: CCD: sweeps shapes precisely (expensive). Speculative: expands contact threshold by velocity, catches fast objects via inflated AABB (cheaper).
questions.correctAnswer: CCD: sweeps shapes precisely (expensive). Speculative: expands contact threshold by velocity, catches fast objects via inflated AABB (cheaper).

questions.question: What is Perlin noise?
options: Gradient-based smooth noise — places random gradient vectors at grid points, interpolates dot products. Natural-looking.
questions.correctAnswer: Gradient-based smooth noise — places random gradient vectors at grid points, interpolates dot products. Natural-looking.
questions.explanation: Ken Perlin 1983. Gradient at each lattice point. Dot with offset vector. Smoothstep interpolation. Zero at integer grid points.

questions.e: Fractal Brownian Motion. Octaves=detail layers. Lacunarity=frequency multiplier. Gain=amplitude衰减. Terrain = 4-8 octaves.
questions.explanation: fBm sums multiple octaves: Σ gainⁱ · noise(lacunarityⁱ · x). Each octave has higher frequency and lower amplitude. lacunarity controls frequency increase (~2.0); gain controls amplitude decrease (~0.5). Used for terrain, clouds, marble.
questions.question: What is fractal noise (fBm)?
options: Sum of multiple noise octaves at different scales: Σ amplitude·noise(frequency·x). Amplitude halves each octave, frequency doubles.
questions.correctAnswer: Sum of multiple noise octaves at different scales: Σ amplitude·noise(frequency·x). Amplitude halves each octave, frequency doubles.

questions.e: Voronoi: random points in cells. F1 = nearest distance. F2-F1 = edge intensity. Produces cellular/crack patterns.
questions.explanation: Worley noise computes distance to the nearest randomly placed feature point within neighboring cells. The result is cellular patterns: stone textures, skin pores, water caustics. F2-F1 gives crack/edge patterns.
questions.question: What is Worley/Voronoi noise?
options: Distance to nearest random feature point — creates cell-like patterns (stone, skin, organic)
questions.correctAnswer: Distance to nearest random feature point — creates cell-like patterns (stone, skin, organic)

questions.e: warp(x,y) = noise(x + noise(x+a,y+b), y + noise(x+c,y+d)). Distorted input → organic swirls. Used for smoke, clouds, lava.
questions.explanation: Domain warping feeds distorted coordinates into a noise function: warp(x,y) = noise(x + noise1, y + noise2). The inner noise displaces the sampling position of the outer noise, creating flowing, organic, marble-like patterns.
questions.question: What is domain warping?
options: Apply noise to the INPUT coordinates of another noise — creates flowing, organic, marble-like patterns
questions.correctAnswer: Apply noise to the INPUT coordinates of another noise — creates flowing, organic, marble-like patterns

questions.e: warp(x,y) = noise(x + noise1, y + noise2). Nested noise distortion. Produces swirling, fluid-like textures. Used for smoke, clouds.
questions.explanation: Perlin places random gradient vectors at integer lattice points. Each sample computes dot products with four surrounding gradients, then smoothstep-interpolates. Value noise just interpolates random scalar values — cheaper but has visible grid artifacts.
questions.question: What is domain warping?
options: Apply noise to INPUT coordinates of another noise — creates flowing, organic, marble-like patterns
questions.correctAnswer: Apply noise to INPUT coordinates of another noise — creates flowing, organic, marble-like patterns

questions.question: What is Poisson disc sampling?
options: Points placed with minimum distance r between all pairs, maximally dense. Natural-looking distribution.
questions.correctAnswer: Points placed with minimum distance r between all pairs, maximally dense. Natural-looking distribution.
questions.explanation: Dart-throwing: random candidates in annulus [r,2r]. Accept if no neighbor within r. Used for foliage placement, object scattering.

questions.e: Blue noise spectrum: low energy at low frequencies (no clumping), peak at sampling frequency. Pre-computed textures (64×64, 128×128).
questions.explanation: Stratified sampling divides the domain into equal strata (grid cells) and places one random sample per cell. This guarantees coverage — no large empty regions. Variance is lower than pure random because inter-stratum variation is eliminated.
questions.question: What is blue noise?
options: Point distribution with minimal low-frequency energy — no visible clumping. Optimal for rendering, dithering, scattering.
questions.correctAnswer: Point distribution with minimal low-frequency energy — no visible clumping. Optimal for rendering, dithering, scattering.

questions.e: Stratified = jittered grid. One sample per cell, random position within. Guarantees no large gaps. Used for shadow maps, SSAO, TAA.
questions.explanation: Blue noise has minimal low-frequency energy — no visible clumping patterns across any scale. The Fourier spectrum is flat at high frequencies with a sharp cutoff. Pre-computed blue noise textures are tiled at runtime for cheap high-quality randomness.
questions.question: What is stratified sampling?
options: Divide domain into equal strata (grid cells), place one sample per stratum — guarantees coverage, reduces variance
questions.correctAnswer: Divide domain into equal strata (grid cells), place one sample per stratum — guarantees coverage, reduces variance

questions.e: Stratified = jittered grid. N cells = N samples, one per cell. Reduces variance compared to pure random. Used for anti-aliasing, shadow maps.
questions.explanation: Poisson disc enforces a minimum distance r between all points while maximizing density. Dart-throwing algorithm: generate random candidates in annulus [r,2r], accept if no neighbor within r. Produces naturally scattered distributions.
questions.question: What is jittered/stratified sampling?
options: Divide domain into grid cells, place one random sample per cell — guarantees coverage, no large gaps
questions.correctAnswer: Divide domain into grid cells, place one random sample per cell — guarantees coverage, no large gaps

questions.e: Void-and-cluster produces blue noise textures. Start white noise, iteratively fill voids and break clusters. Pre-compute once, tile at runtime.
questions.explanation: Void-and-cluster iteratively improves a point set: find the largest gap (void) and place a point there; find the tightest cluster and remove a point. Repeated iterations converge to a blue noise distribution. Pre-compute once, use forever.
questions.question: What is the void-and-cluster algorithm?
options: Generates blue noise: finds largest gap (void), places point; finds tightest cluster, removes point. Iterates to convergence.
questions.correctAnswer: Generates blue noise: finds largest gap (void), places point; finds tightest cluster, removes point. Iterates to convergence.

questions.question: What is an L-system?
options: A parallel string rewriting system — axiom + production rules → geometric interpretation via turtle graphics
questions.correctAnswer: A parallel string rewriting system — axiom + production rules → geometric interpretation via turtle graphics
questions.explanation: Lindenmayer 1968. Axiom→rule application→string→turtle graphics. F=draw, +=turn, []=push/pop for branching. Generates plants, trees.

questions.e: Marching Cubes: for each voxel, 8 corners inside/outside → 256 configs → triangle vertices along edges. 15 unique topological cases.
questions.explanation: Turtle graphics interprets the L-system string: F draws a line forward, + turns right by angle δ, - turns left, [ pushes current position/heading onto a stack, ] pops to return. Branches are created by pushing before a side branch and popping to return to the main stem.
questions.question: What is Marching Cubes?
options: Extracts isosurface from 3D scalar field — classifies 8 corners of each voxel, indexes 256-entry lookup table for triangles
questions.correctAnswer: Extracts isosurface from 3D scalar field — classifies 8 corners of each voxel, indexes 256-entry lookup table for triangles

questions.e: Marching Cubes doesn't care about the source. SDF = exact distance. Noise = organic terrain. Metaballs = blobby merging. All produce valid meshes.
questions.explanation: Marching Cubes processes each voxel independently: 8 corners × 2 states (inside/outside) = 256 possible configurations. Rotational symmetry reduces these to 15 unique topological cases. A lookup table maps each case to the triangle vertices needed.
questions.question: What is the scalar field input to Marching Cubes?
options: Any continuous function R³→R — distance field, noise, metaballs, implicit surfaces. Isosurface at f(x,y,z)=threshold.
questions.correctAnswer: Any continuous function R³→R — distance field, noise, metaballs, implicit surfaces. Isosurface at f(x,y,z)=threshold.

questions.e: Turtle: pen with position and heading. F draws line. [ saves position/heading on stack, ] restores. Enables branching structures.
questions.explanation: The scalar field can be any continuous function f(x,y,z): signed distance field, Perlin noise, metaballs, implicit surfaces. Marching Cubes extracts the isosurface where f=threshold. The function determines the shape; Marching Cubes generates the mesh.
questions.question: What is the turtle graphics interpretation of an L-system?
options: F=draw forward, +=turn right, -=turn left, []=push state, ]=pop state. Converts L-string to geometry.
questions.correctAnswer: F=draw forward, +=turn right, -=turn left, []=push state, ]=pop state. Converts L-string to geometry.

questions.e: 8 corners × 2 states = 256. Rotation/reflection reduces to 15 unique topological cases. Lookup table maps case→triangle list.
questions.explanation: 256 total configurations (2⁸). By rotational and reflection symmetry, only 15 are topologically distinct. Case 0: all corners outside (no triangles). Case 255: all inside (no triangles). Cases 1-14: varying numbers of triangles along edges where sign changes.
questions.question: What are the 15 unique cases in Marching Cubes?
options: 256 voxel configurations reduce to 15 by rotational symmetry. Each case defines triangle vertices along cube edges.
questions.correctAnswer: 256 voxel configurations reduce to 15 by rotational symmetry. Each case defines triangle vertices along cube edges.

questions.question: What is importance sampling?
options: Drawing samples from a distribution proportional to where the integrand is large — reduces variance
questions.correctAnswer: Drawing samples from a distribution proportional to where the integrand is large — reduces variance
questions.explanation: Instead of uniform sampling (wastes samples on low-contribution regions), sample where it matters. Essential for Monte Carlo rendering.

questions.e: CDF = cumulative distribution. Invert analytically or numerically. Sample uniform→apply inverse→get sample from target distribution.
questions.explanation: Pseudo-random: each sample independent (clumps and gaps). Quasi-random (Sobol, Halton): low-discrepancy — samples are evenly distributed, filling space more uniformly. Quasi-random converges faster for Monte Carlo integration (~O(1/N) vs O(1/√N)).
questions.question: What is the inversion method for sampling?
options: Compute CDF, invert it, sample uniform [0,1] → CDF⁻¹(uniform). Generates samples from arbitrary distribution.
questions.correctAnswer: Compute CDF, invert it, sample uniform [0,1] → CDF⁻¹(uniform). Generates samples from arbitrary distribution.

questions.e: Quasi-random = deterministic, evenly spaced. Better convergence for Monte Carlo integration. Sobol sequence used in UE5 Lightmass.
questions.explanation: Importance sampling draws samples from a distribution p(x) proportional to the integrand. This puts more samples where the function is large, reducing variance. For rendering: sample lights more than dark void; sample specular lobe tightly.
questions.question: What is the difference between pseudo-random and quasi-random sequences?
options: Pseudo-random: each sample independent (clumps). Quasi-random (Sobol, Halton): low-discrepancy — samples fill space evenly.
questions.correctAnswer: Pseudo-random: each sample independent (clumps). Quasi-random (Sobol, Halton): low-discrepancy — samples fill space evenly.

questions.e: Instead of uniform (wastes samples on darkness), sample where light is. Cosine-weighted hemisphere, GGX specular lobe.
questions.explanation: Metropolis-Hastings is a Markov Chain Monte Carlo method: propose a random move from current sample, accept with probability min(1, target_pdf(proposed)/target_pdf(current)). The random walk converges to the target distribution. Handles complex PDFs.
questions.question: What is importance sampling?
options: Drawing samples proportional to where the integrand is large — reduces variance without increasing sample count
questions.correctAnswer: Drawing samples proportional to where the integrand is large — reduces variance without increasing sample count

questions.e: Metropolis: random walk that converges to target distribution. Proposal + acceptance probability. Handles distributions where direct sampling is hard.
questions.explanation: The inversion method: compute the CDF (cumulative distribution), invert it, then sample uniform[0,1] → apply CDF⁻¹. The output follows the target distribution. Works when the CDF is analytically invertible or can be numerically inverted.
questions.question: What is Metropolis-Hastings sampling?
options: MCMC: generates samples from a distribution by proposing moves and accepting/rejecting based on probability ratio. Good for complex PDFs.
questions.correctAnswer: MCMC: generates samples from a distribution by proposing moves and accepting/rejecting based on probability ratio. Good for complex PDFs.

questions.question: What is an octree?
options: 3D spatial partitioning: recursively subdivide space into 8 octants. Each node has 0 or 8 children.
questions.correctAnswer: 3D spatial partitioning: recursively subdivide space into 8 octants. Each node has 0 or 8 children.
questions.explanation: Octree = 3D quadtree. Used for spatial indexing (collision, rendering), level-of-detail, voxel compression. UE uses for lightmass and some collision.

questions.e: VOlumetric piXEL. Minecraft-style worlds, medical CT scans, UE Voxel Plugin. Marching Cubes converts voxels to meshes.
questions.explanation: An octree recursively subdivides 3D space into 8 octants. Each node has either 0 children (leaf) or 8 children (subdivided). Used for spatial indexing — only subdivide cells containing objects. UE lightmass, collision queries, and voxel plugins use octrees.
questions.question: What are voxels?
options: 3D pixels — volumetric elements. Store density, material, color per cell. Used for terrain, medical imaging, UE Voxel Plugin.
questions.correctAnswer: 3D pixels — volumetric elements. Store density, material, color per cell. Used for terrain, medical imaging, UE Voxel Plugin.

questions.e: Each node: 0 or 8 children. Subdivide when cell has too many objects. UE lightmass, some collision queries, voxel plugins all use octrees.
questions.explanation: SVO only allocates nodes for regions containing data. Vast empty spaces have zero nodes. Memory usage scales with surface area (~O(n²)) rather than volume (~O(n³)). Enables high-resolution voxel data (e.g., 4096³) that would be impossible with dense grids.
questions.question: What is an octree used for?
options: Spatial indexing: 3D recursive subdivision into 8 octants. Used for collision, LOD, voxel compression, light mapping
questions.correctAnswer: Spatial indexing: 3D recursive subdivision into 8 octants. Used for collision, LOD, voxel compression, light mapping

questions.e: SVO: only allocate where geometry exists. Vast empty spaces = no nodes. Enables high-resolution voxel data without memory explosion.
questions.explanation: DDA (Digital Differential Analyzer) steps ray through voxel grid: compute t to next X, Y, and Z boundaries; advance to the closest one. Each step is O(1). Total cost = O(number of voxels traversed). Used in voxel ray tracing and Minecraft-style rendering.
questions.question: How does sparse voxel octree (SVO) save memory?
options: Only store nodes that contain data — skip empty regions. Dense grid = O(n³); SVO = O(surface area).
questions.correctAnswer: Only store nodes that contain data — skip empty regions. Dense grid = O(n³); SVO = O(surface area).

questions.e: DDA (Digital Differential Analyzer): advance ray to next voxel boundary. O(number of voxels traversed). Used in voxel ray tracing, Minecraft-style games.
questions.explanation: A voxel represents a value on a regular 3D grid — the 3D analog of a pixel. Each voxel stores data (density, material ID, color). Marching Cubes converts voxel data to polygon meshes. Used in medical imaging, terrain generation, UE Voxel Plugin.
questions.question: What is ray-voxel traversal?
options: Marching ray through voxel grid: step to next voxel boundary along each axis. Efficient for ray casting in voxel worlds.
questions.correctAnswer: Marching ray through voxel grid: step to next voxel boundary along each axis. Efficient for ray casting in voxel worlds.

questions.question: What is the graphics pipeline from vertices to pixels?
options: Vertex shader → rasterizer → pixel shader → output merger. Programmable stages (shaders) + fixed-function (rasterizer).
questions.correctAnswer: Vertex shader → rasterizer → pixel shader → output merger. Programmable stages (shaders) + fixed-function (rasterizer).
questions.explanation: Vertex shader: transform vertices. Rasterizer: generate fragments. Pixel shader: compute color. Output merger: depth test, blend. UE's RHI abstracts this.

questions.e: Fixed-function = non-programmable stages. Rasterizer: generates fragments from triangles. Output merger: depth/stencil test, blending. Extremely fast dedicated silicon.
questions.explanation: The rasterizer is fixed-function hardware between vertex and pixel shaders. It generates fragments for each covered pixel, interpolates vertex attributes (UV, color, normal) using barycentric coordinates, and performs early depth testing.
questions.question: What does fixed-function hardware do in the GPU pipeline?
options: Rasterizer (triangles→fragments), output merger (depth test, blend) — not programmable, just configurable
questions.correctAnswer: Rasterizer (triangles→fragments), output merger (depth test, blend) — not programmable, just configurable
Vertex shader, hull shader, domain shader, geometry shader, pixel shader, compute shader
acceptableAnswers: vertex shader pixel shader compute shader
questions.explanation: Vertex: transform vertices. Pixel: color fragments. Compute: arbitrary parallel work. Hull/Domain: tessellation. Geometry: primitive manipulation.
questions.question: What are the programmable stages of the GPU pipeline?

questions.e: Rasterizer = fixed-function. Triangle setup → edge walking → fragment generation. Interpolates depth, UV, color, normal per fragment.
questions.explanation: The vertex shader transforms vertices (model→world→camera→clip), the pixel/fragment shader computes per-pixel color (lighting, texturing), and compute shaders bypass the graphics pipeline entirely for arbitrary GPU computation.
questions.question: What happens in the rasterizer stage?
options: Converts triangles to pixel fragments — determines which pixels are covered, interpolates vertex attributes across triangle
questions.correctAnswer: Converts triangles to pixel fragments — determines which pixels are covered, interpolates vertex attributes across triangle

questions.e: Render target = output surface. Color buffer, depth buffer, stencil. Deferred rendering uses multiple targets (GBuffer).
questions.explanation: A render target is a GPU memory buffer that shaders write to. It can be the screen framebuffer, a texture (render-to-texture), or multiple targets in deferred rendering (GBuffer: albedo, normal, depth, etc.).
questions.question: What is a render target?
options: Memory buffer GPU renders into — can be framebuffer (screen), texture, or multiple targets (MRT).
questions.correctAnswer: Memory buffer GPU renders into — can be framebuffer (screen), texture, or multiple targets (MRT).

questions.question: What is FName?
options: Interned, immutable string — stored in global table, O(1) comparison via index
questions.correctAnswer: Interned, immutable string — stored in global table, O(1) comparison via index
questions.explanation: FName: hash table index. Comparing two FNames = comparing integers. Used for asset paths, identifiers. Case-insensitive.

questions.e: FText = user-facing, localizable. FString = mutable, gameplay use. FName = interned, identifiers. Three distinct use cases.
questions.explanation: FName is an index into the global TNameEntryArray table. operator== compares two integers (table indices), not strings. This O(1) comparison makes FNames ideal for asset paths, identifiers, and gameplay tags where comparison speed matters.
questions.question: What is FText?
options: Localizable display text — supports translation, pluralization. Used for UI. Not for gameplay logic.
questions.correctAnswer: Localizable display text — supports translation, pluralization. Used for UI. Not for gameplay logic.

questions.e: TCHAR abstracts platform encoding. TEXT("hello") = L"hello" on Windows, "hello" elsewhere. FString is TArray<TCHAR>.
questions.explanation: FText wraps a display string with localization support: translation tables, pluralization rules, culture-specific formatting. Used for UI text that players see. FString is for gameplay logic; FName is for identifiers. Never mix them.
questions.question: What is TCHAR?
options: UE's character type — wchar_t on Windows (UTF-16), char on others. TEXT() macro ensures correct encoding.
questions.correctAnswer: UE's character type — wchar_t on Windows (UTF-16), char on others. TEXT() macro ensures correct encoding.

questions.e: FName = (index, number). Index into global TNameEntryArray. == operator compares indices. String comparison is never done at runtime.
questions.explanation: The FName pool is a fixed-size hash table created at engine startup. All FNames exist forever — you cannot remove them. Creating FNames from runtime-generated strings (player names, chat messages) would eventually overflow the pool. Use FString for dynamic text.
questions.question: Why is FName comparison O(1)?
options: FName stores an index into a global name table — comparing two FNames is comparing two integers (table indices)
questions.correctAnswer: FName stores an index into a global name table — comparing two FNames is comparing two integers (table indices)

questions.e: FNames live forever in the global pool. Don't create FNames from dynamic strings (player names, chat messages). Use FString for runtime-generated text.
questions.explanation: TCHAR abstracts platform character encoding. On Windows: wchar_t (UTF-16). On other platforms: char (UTF-8). The TEXT() macro ensures string literals have the correct encoding. FString = TArray<TCHAR> internally.
questions.question: What is the FName pool and why does it matter?
options: A fixed-size hash table of all FNames ever created. Can't be cleared at runtime. Too many unique FNames = overflow (assert).
questions.correctAnswer: A fixed-size hash table of all FNames ever created. Can't be cleared at runtime. Too many unique FNames = overflow (assert).

questions.question: Why does UE use MoveTemp instead of std::move?
options: UE wrapper — same semantics but consistent naming convention. MoveTemp(x) ≡ std::move(x).
questions.correctAnswer: UE wrapper — same semantics but consistent naming convention. MoveTemp(x) ≡ std::move(x).
questions.explanation: UE naming convention: MoveTemp for rvalue cast. MoveTempIfPossible for conditional move. All wrap standard move semantics.

questions.e: MoveTemp = UE naming for std::move. MoveTempIfPossible = conditional move. Same underlying mechanics as standard move semantics.
questions.explanation: MoveTempIfPossible is UE's conditional move: if T has a move constructor, cast to rvalue (enable move); otherwise leave as lvalue (copy). Similar to std::move_if_noexcept but UE-specific. Used in TArray growth and container operations.
questions.question: What does MoveTemp do?
options: UE's std::move — casts to rvalue reference. MoveTemp(x) ≡ static_cast<T&&>(x). Enables move semantics.
questions.correctAnswer: UE's std::move — casts to rvalue reference. MoveTemp(x) ≡ static_cast<T&&>(x). Enables move semantics.

questions.e: MoveTempIfPossible checks TIsMoveConstructible<T>. Falls back to copy for non-movable types. Used in TArray growth path.
questions.explanation: UE disables C++ exceptions globally. Without exceptions, there's no exception-safety concern during move. TArray can always move elements on reallocation without worrying about a partial move leaving the array corrupted if an exception is thrown.
questions.question: What does MoveTempIfPossible do?
options: Conditionally moves if T is movable, otherwise copies. Similar to std::move_if_noexcept but UE-specific.
questions.correctAnswer: Conditionally moves if T is movable, otherwise copies. Similar to std::move_if_noexcept but UE-specific.

questions.e: No exceptions = no exception-safety concern. TArray doesn't need move_if_noexcept. Always moves elements on reallocation.
questions.explanation: FORCEINLINE expands to __forceinline (MSVC) or __attribute__((always_inline)) (Clang/GCC). MoveTemp is a tiny template that MUST be zero-cost — forcing inlining guarantees there's no function call overhead for what should just be a cast.
questions.question: Why is TArray always able to move on reallocation?
options: UE disables exceptions globally — every type is effectively noexcept-move. TArray always moves, never falls back to copy.
questions.correctAnswer: UE disables exceptions globally — every type is effectively noexcept-move. TArray always moves, never falls back to copy.

questions.e: MoveTemp is a template that MUST be inlined. FORCEINLINE = __forceinline on MSVC, always_inline on Clang. Zero function call overhead.
questions.explanation: MoveTemp(x) ≡ static_cast<typename TRemoveReference<T>::Type&&>(x). It's a cast — it doesn't move anything. It enables the move constructor or move assignment operator to be selected by overload resolution. Same mechanism as std::move.
questions.question: What is FORCEINLINE and why is it used on MoveTemp?
options: Forces inlining even in debug builds — MoveTemp must be zero-cost. Essential for performance-critical paths.
questions.correctAnswer: Forces inlining even in debug builds — MoveTemp must be zero-cost. Essential for performance-critical paths.

questions.question: What is TObjectPtr?
options: GC-tracked smart pointer for UObjects — transparent replacement for raw T* with lazy loading and editor integration
questions.correctAnswer: GC-tracked smart pointer for UObjects — transparent replacement for raw T* with lazy loading and editor integration
questions.explanation: TObjectPtr<T> replaces raw T* in UE5. Implicit conversion to T*. GC tracking. Lazy object resolution. Editor cook integration.

questions.e: TSharedPtr: non-UObject ref-counting. TSharedRef: non-nullable variant. TWeakPtr: non-owning observer. For UObjects use TObjectPtr.
questions.explanation: TSharedPtr is reference-counted (like std::shared_ptr) for non-UObject types (FVector, custom structs). UObjects use UE's garbage collector — adding shared_ptr refcounting would conflict with GC. Use TSharedPtr for plain C++ objects.
questions.question: What is TSharedPtr?
options: Reference-counted smart pointer for NON-UObject types — similar to std::shared_ptr
questions.correctAnswer: Reference-counted smart pointer for NON-UObject types — similar to std::shared_ptr

questions.e: TUniquePtr<T> = exclusive heap ownership. Cannot be copied. Move transfers ownership. Automatically deletes on destruction.
questions.explanation: TUniquePtr provides exclusive heap ownership for non-UObject types. Cannot be copied (deleted copy constructor). Moving transfers ownership. Automatically calls delete on destruction. UE's equivalent of std::unique_ptr.
questions.question: What is TUniquePtr?
options: Exclusive ownership — UE's std::unique_ptr. Non-copyable, movable. For non-UObject types.
questions.correctAnswer: Exclusive ownership — UE's std::unique_ptr. Non-copyable, movable. For non-UObject types.

questions.e: TWeakObjectPtr = observer. Doesn't keep UObject alive. Used for optional references, back-pointers that shouldn't prevent GC.
questions.explanation: TWeakObjectPtr observes a UObject without preventing GC. Must call IsValid() before dereferencing — the object may have been garbage collected. Used for optional references, back-pointers, and cached lookups that shouldn't extend lifetime.
questions.question: What is TWeakObjectPtr?
options: Non-owning reference to UObject — doesn't prevent GC. Must check IsValid() before use.
questions.correctAnswer: Non-owning reference to UObject — doesn't prevent GC. Must check IsValid() before use.

questions.e: UObject lifetime is managed by UE's garbage collector. Adding shared_ptr refcounting would create two competing ownership systems.
questions.explanation: UObjects have their own mark-and-sweep garbage collector. std::shared_ptr tracks reference counts independently — the two systems would compete and potentially double-delete or leak. UE provides TObjectPtr for GC-tracked ownership instead.
questions.question: Why can't you use std::shared_ptr for UObjects?
options: UObjects have their own GC — std::shared_ptr's reference counting would conflict. UE GC manages UObject lifetime.
questions.correctAnswer: UObjects have their own GC — std::shared_ptr's reference counting would conflict. UE GC manages UObject lifetime.

questions.question: What is TArray's growth strategy?
options: Similar to std::vector — exponential growth (UE adds slack on reallocation). TArray::Add() reallocates when full.
questions.correctAnswer: Similar to std::vector — exponential growth (UE adds slack on reallocation). TArray::Add() reallocates when full.
questions.explanation: TArray = dynamic array. Similar to vector. Grow by adding slack (default 4 elements at small sizes). Reserve() prevents reallocation.

questions.e: Emplace like emplace_back. Avoids temporary creation. TArray::Emplace(Args...) forwards to element constructor at new end position.
questions.explanation: Reserve(N) pre-allocates capacity to at least N elements. Subsequent Add() calls don't reallocate until size exceeds N. Use when you know the final size upfront — avoids all reallocation overhead during population.
questions.question: What does TArray::Emplace do vs Add?
options: Emplace constructs in-place at end — forwards args to constructor. Add copies/moves existing object. Emplace avoids temporary.
questions.correctAnswer: Emplace constructs in-place at end — forwards args to constructor. Add copies/moves existing object. Emplace avoids temporary.

questions.e: Reserve(N) = allocate N slots upfront. Push N elements without reallocation. Same as std::vector::reserve.
questions.explanation: UE's TArray adds Slack (default=4) plus max(old_capacity, Slack) on each growth. Small arrays grow by a few elements; large arrays grow proportionally. This amortized O(1) growth is similar to std::vector's exponential strategy.
questions.question: What does TArray::Reserve do?
options: Pre-allocates capacity to at least N — prevents reallocation during subsequent Adds
questions.correctAnswer: Pre-allocates capacity to at least N — prevents reallocation during subsequent Adds

questions.e: UE adds Slack (default=4) each growth. Also grows by old_capacity. Larger arrays grow faster. Amortized O(1) Add like std::vector.
questions.explanation: TArray::SetNum(N) resizes to exactly N elements. If N > current size: default-constructs new elements (or uses specified value). If N < current size: destroys excess elements. Does NOT change capacity — only changes the logical size.
questions.question: What is TArray's growth strategy when full?
options: Adds slack: new_capacity = old_capacity + max(old_capacity, Slack). Exponential growth for amortized O(1) Add.
questions.correctAnswer: Adds slack: new_capacity = old_capacity + max(old_capacity, Slack). Exponential growth for amortized O(1) Add.

questions.e: SetNum(N) = resize to N. If N > size: default-construct new elements. If N < size: destroy excess elements. Does NOT change capacity.
questions.explanation: Emplace forwards constructor arguments directly to the new element's constructor at the end of the array — no temporary object, no copy, no move. Same as std::vector::emplace_back. Use Emplace(args...) when constructing complex objects in-place.
questions.question: What does TArray::SetNum do?
options: Resizes array to N elements — initializes new elements (default or specified value), destroys excess
questions.correctAnswer: Resizes array to N elements — initializes new elements (default or specified value), destroys excess

questions.question: What data structure does TMap use?
options: Hash table with separate chaining — O(1) average, similar to std::unordered_map
questions.correctAnswer: Hash table with separate chaining — O(1) average, similar to std::unordered_map
questions.explanation: TMap = unordered hash map. Uses sparse array with chaining. Key must have GetTypeHash(). No ordering guarantees. O(1) average lookup.

questions.e: FVector* val = Map.Find(key); if(val) use(*val); Standard UE pattern — pointer-based lookup, no exceptions.
questions.explanation: TMap uses GetTypeHash(var T&) to compute the hash bucket. Built-in types (int, float, FString, FName) have predefined GetTypeHash. Custom structs must provide a specialization: friend uint32 GetTypeHash(var FMyStruct& S).
questions.question: What does TMap::Find do?
options: Returns pointer to value if key exists, nullptr otherwise. No exception.
questions.correctAnswer: Returns pointer to value if key exists, nullptr otherwise. No exception.

questions.e: TMap requires GetTypeHash(var T&). Built-in types, FString, FName have it. Custom structs: add friend uint32 GetTypeHash(var FMyStruct&).
questions.explanation: FindOrAdd returns a reference to the value for the given key. If the key doesn't exist, it inserts a default-constructed value and returns a reference to it. Useful pattern: TMap[key].Count++ or auto& Val = Map.FindOrAdd(Key); Val = ComputeValue().
questions.question: What hash function does TMap require?
options: GetTypeHash() — must be defined for key type. UE provides for built-in types. Custom types need specialization.
questions.correctAnswer: GetTypeHash() — must be defined for key type. UE provides for built-in types. Custom types need specialization.

questions.e: FindOrAdd: returns existing value or creates new entry. Useful for counters, caches. TMap[Key] = TMap::FindOrAdd(Key) + 1 style patterns.
